The official and *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* flag of Northern Ireland is the [Union Jack](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack").[[10]](#cite_note-13) The [Ulster Banner](/wiki/Ulster_Banner "Ulster Banner") was used by the [Parliament of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Northern_Ireland "Parliament of Northern Ireland") from 1953 until the latter was abolished in 1973. The Ulster Banner is still used by some organisations and entities and is used to represent Northern Ireland when it plays as a national sports team. See [Northern Ireland flags issue](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_flags_issue "Northern Ireland flags issue") for more. | |

**Northern Ireland**[[d]](#cite_note-15) is a [part](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom") of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") in the north-east of the island of [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"). It has been [variously described](#Descriptions) as a country, province or region.[[12]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-16)[[13]](#cite_note-ISO-17)[[14]](#cite_note-alphabeticalNI-18)[[15]](#cite_note-interpretingNI-19)[[16]](#cite_note-placeApart-20) Northern Ireland shares [an open border](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland%E2%80%93United_Kingdom_border "Republic of Ireland–United Kingdom border") to the south and west with the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland"). At the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census"), its population was 1,903,175,[[7]](#cite_note-NISRA_2021_census-9) making up around 3% of the [UK's population](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_United_Kingdom#Population "Demographics of the United Kingdom") and 27% of the population on the island of [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland#Demographics "Ireland"). The [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly"), established by the [Northern Ireland Act 1998](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Act_1998 "Northern Ireland Act 1998"), holds responsibility for a range of [devolved](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") policy matters, while other areas are reserved for the [UK Government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom"). The [government of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Government_of_Northern_Ireland "Government of Northern Ireland") cooperates with the [government of Ireland](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland "Government of Ireland") in several areas under the terms of the [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement"). The Republic of Ireland also has a consultative role on non-devolved governmental matters through the [British–Irish Intergovernmental Conference](/wiki/British%E2%80%93Irish_Intergovernmental_Conference "British–Irish Intergovernmental Conference").[[17]](#cite_note-21)

Northern Ireland was created in 1921, when [Ireland was partitioned](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland") by the [Government of Ireland Act 1920](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920 "Government of Ireland Act 1920"), creating a devolved government for the [six northeastern counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Northern_Ireland "Counties of Northern Ireland"). As was intended by unionists and their supporters in [Westminster](/wiki/Westminster "Westminster"), Northern Ireland had a [unionist](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland") majority, who wanted to remain in the United Kingdom;[[18]](#cite_note-McKittrick-McVea_p5-22) they were generally the [Protestant](/wiki/Ulster_Protestants "Ulster Protestants") descendants of [colonists from Britain](/wiki/Plantation_of_Ulster "Plantation of Ulster"). Meanwhile, the majority in [Southern Ireland](/wiki/Southern_Ireland_(1921%E2%80%931922) "Southern Ireland (1921–1922)") (which became the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") in 1922), and a significant minority in Northern Ireland, were [Irish nationalists](/wiki/Irish_nationalism "Irish nationalism") (generally [Catholics](/wiki/Irish_Catholics "Irish Catholics")) who wanted a [united independent Ireland](/wiki/United_Ireland "United Ireland").[[19]](#cite_note-23) Today, the former generally see themselves as British and the latter generally see themselves as Irish, while a Northern Irish or [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster") identity is claimed by a significant minority from all backgrounds.[[20]](#cite_note-24)

The creation of Northern Ireland was marred by violence between those for and against partition. During [The Troubles in Ulster (1920–1922)](/wiki/The_Troubles_in_Ulster_(1920%E2%80%931922) "The Troubles in Ulster (1920–1922)"), the capital [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast") saw major [communal violence](/wiki/Communal_violence "Communal violence"), mainly between Protestant unionist and Catholic nationalist civilians.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynch201911,_100–101-25) More than 500 were killed[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynch201999-26) and more than 10,000 became refugees, mostly Catholics.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynch2019171–176-27) For the next fifty years, Northern Ireland had an unbroken series of [Unionist Party governments](/wiki/Government_of_Northern_Ireland_(1921%E2%80%931972) "Government of Northern Ireland (1921–1972)").[[24]](#cite_note-28) There was informal mutual [segregation](/wiki/Segregation_in_Northern_Ireland "Segregation in Northern Ireland") by both communities,[[25]](#cite_note-29) and the Unionist governments were accused of discrimination against the Irish nationalist and Catholic minority.[[26]](#cite_note-30) In the late 1960s, a [campaign to end discrimination](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Civil_Rights_Association "Northern Ireland Civil Rights Association") against Catholics and nationalists was opposed by [loyalists](/wiki/Ulster_loyalism "Ulster loyalism"), who saw it as a [republican](/wiki/Irish_republicanism "Irish republicanism") front.[[27]](#cite_note-31) This unrest sparked [the Troubles](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles"), a thirty-year conflict involving republican and loyalist paramilitaries and state forces, which claimed over 3,500 lives and injured 50,000 others.[[28]](#cite_note-32)[[29]](#cite_note-auto2-33) The 1998 [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement") was a major step in the [peace process](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_peace_process "Northern Ireland peace process"), including [paramilitary disarmament](/wiki/Decommissioning_in_Northern_Ireland "Decommissioning in Northern Ireland") and security normalisation, although [sectarianism](/wiki/Sectarianism "Sectarianism") and segregation remain major social problems, and sporadic violence has continued.[[30]](#cite_note-34)

The [economy of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Economy_of_Northern_Ireland "Economy of Northern Ireland") was the most industrialised in Ireland at the time of partition, but soon began to decline, exacerbated by the political and social turmoil of the Troubles.[[31]](#cite_note-35) Its economy has grown significantly since the late 1990s. Unemployment in Northern Ireland peaked at 17.2% in 1986, but dropped back down to below 10% in the 2010s,[[32]](#cite_note-36) similar to the rate of the rest of the UK.[[33]](#cite_note-37) Cultural links between Northern Ireland, the rest of Ireland, and the rest of the UK are complex, with Northern Ireland sharing both the [culture of Ireland](/wiki/Culture_of_Ireland "Culture of Ireland") and the [culture of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Culture of the United Kingdom"). In many sports, there is an [All-Ireland](/wiki/All-Ireland "All-Ireland") governing body or team for the whole island; the most notable exception is association football. Northern Ireland [competes separately](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_at_the_Commonwealth_Games "Northern Ireland at the Commonwealth Games") at the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"), and people from Northern Ireland may compete for either [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_Olympics "Great Britain at the Olympics") or [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_at_the_Olympics "Ireland at the Olympics") at the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games").

## History

Main article: [History of Northern Ireland](/wiki/History_of_Northern_Ireland "History of Northern Ireland")

See also: [History of Ireland](/wiki/History_of_Ireland "History of Ireland") and [Ulster § History](/wiki/Ulster#History "Ulster")

[![Northern Ireland – Counties](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Northern_Ireland_-_Counties.png/250px-Northern_Ireland_-_Counties.png)](/wiki/File:Northern_Ireland_-_Counties.png)

The traditional counties of Northern Ireland

The region now known as Northern Ireland was historically inhabited by Irish-speaking [Gaels](/wiki/Gaels "Gaels").[[34]](#cite_note-38) It consisted of several Gaelic kingdoms within the province of [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster"). In 1169, Ireland was invaded by [Anglo-Norman](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_invasion_of_Ireland "Anglo-Norman invasion of Ireland") forces under the English crown, initiating centuries of foreign dominance. While English authority spread across much of Ireland, Ulster's major Gaelic kingdoms such as the [Uí Néill](/wiki/U%C3%AD_N%C3%A9ill "Uí Néill") largely retained their autonomy, with English control limited to parts of the eastern coast.

During the [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War_(Ireland) "Nine Years' War (Ireland)") (1593–1603), an alliance of Gaelic chieftains led by [Hugh Roe O'Donnell](/wiki/Hugh_Roe_O%27Donnell "Hugh Roe O'Donnell") and [Hugh O'Neill](/wiki/Hugh_O%27Neill,_Earl_of_Tyrone "Hugh O'Neill, Earl of Tyrone") resisted English rule. Despite early successes and support from Spain, the alliance was ultimately defeated, culminating in the [Flight of the Earls](/wiki/Flight_of_the_Earls "Flight of the Earls") in 1607, when many Ulster nobles fled to [mainland Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe"). Their lands were confiscated and colonized by English-speaking Protestant settlers from Britain in the [Plantation of Ulster](/wiki/Plantation_of_Ulster "Plantation of Ulster"), establishing a lasting Protestant community with ties to Britain.[[35]](#cite_note-39)

The [Irish Rebellion of 1641](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1641 "Irish Rebellion of 1641") began in Ulster, driven by demands to end [anti-Catholic](/wiki/Anti-Catholicism "Anti-Catholicism") discrimination, achieve greater Irish self-governance, and reverse the plantations. The uprising escalated into an ethnic conflict between [Irish Catholics](/wiki/Irish_Catholics "Irish Catholics") and British Protestant settlers and became part of the wider [Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms") (1639–1653), concluding with the [English Parliamentarian](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") conquest

Subsequent Protestant victories in the [Williamite-Jacobite War](/wiki/Williamite_War_in_Ireland "Williamite War in Ireland") (1688–1691), including the [Siege of Derry](/wiki/Siege_of_Derry "Siege of Derry") and the [Battle of the Boyne](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Boyne "Battle of the Boyne"), solidified Anglican Protestant rule in Ireland. These events are still commemorated by some Protestants in Northern Ireland.[[36]](#cite_note-40) Many more Scots Protestants migrated to Ulster during the [Scottish famine of the 1690s](/wiki/Seven_ill_years "Seven ill years").

Following the Williamite victory, a series of [Penal Laws](/wiki/Penal_laws_(Ireland) "Penal laws (Ireland)") were enacted by the Anglican Protestant ruling class to suppress Catholicism and, to a lesser extent, [Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism"). These laws aimed to concentrate property and public office in the hands of those aligned with the [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland").[[37]](#cite_note-41)[[38]](#cite_note-42)[[39]](#cite_note-43) Between 1717 and 1775, approximately 200,000 [Ulster Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Ireland "Presbyterian Church in Ireland") emigrated to the American colonies, where their descendants are known as [Scotch-Irish Americans](/wiki/Scotch-Irish_Americans "Scotch-Irish Americans").[[40]](#cite_note-44)[[41]](#cite_note-45)

In the late 18th century, secretive and militant societies emerged in Ulster, exacerbating sectarian tensions. The [Protestant Peep o' Day Boys](/wiki/Peep_o%27_Day_Boys "Peep o' Day Boys") and the Catholic Defenders frequently clashed, notably during the [Armagh disturbances](/wiki/Armagh_disturbances "Armagh disturbances"), culminating in the [Battle of the Diamond](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Diamond "Battle of the Diamond") in 1795.[[42]](#cite_note-46) This event led to the founding of the [Protestant Orange Order](/wiki/Orange_Order "Orange Order").[[43]](#cite_note-47)[[44]](#cite_note-48)

The Irish Rebellion of 1798 was led by the [Society of United Irishmen](/wiki/Society_of_United_Irishmen "Society of United Irishmen"), a cross-community republican group founded by Belfast Presbyterians seeking Irish independence.[[45]](#cite_note-49) The rebellion was ultimately suppressed, but it highlighted the growing demand for reform.[[46]](#cite_note-50) In response, the British government enacted the [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800"), merging the Kingdom of Ireland with Great Britain to form the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland in 1801. This legislative union aimed to quell sectarianism, remove discriminatory laws, and prevent the spread of revolutionary ideas.[[47]](#cite_note-51)

### Home Rule Crisis

Main article: [Home Rule crisis](/wiki/Home_Rule_crisis "Home Rule crisis")

See also: [Unionism in Ireland § "The Ulster Option" 1905–1920](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland#"The_Ulster_Option"_1905–1920 "Unionism in Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/de/Carson_signing_Solemn_League_and_Covenant.jpg/250px-Carson_signing_Solemn_League_and_Covenant.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carson_signing_Solemn_League_and_Covenant.jpg)

Signing of the [Ulster Covenant](/wiki/Ulster_Covenant "Ulster Covenant") in 1912 in opposition to Home Rule

By the late 19th century, a large and disciplined cohort of [Irish Nationalist](/wiki/Irish_Parliamentary_Party "Irish Parliamentary Party") MPs at Westminster committed the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") to ["Irish Home Rule"](/wiki/Irish_Home_Rule_movement "Irish Home Rule movement")—self-government for Ireland, within the United Kingdom. This was bitterly opposed by [Irish Unionists](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland"), most of whom were Protestants, who feared an Irish devolved government dominated by Irish nationalists and Catholics. The [Government of Ireland Bill 1886](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Bill_1886 "Government of Ireland Bill 1886") and [Government of Ireland Bill 1893](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Bill_1893 "Government of Ireland Bill 1893") were defeated. However, Home Rule became a near-certainty in 1912 after the [Government of Ireland Act 1914](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Act_1914 "Government of Ireland Act 1914") was first introduced. The Liberal government was dependent on Nationalist support, and the [Parliament Act 1911](/wiki/Parliament_Act_1911 "Parliament Act 1911") prevented the House of Lords from blocking the bill indefinitely.[[48]](#cite_note-Lydon326-52)

In response, unionists vowed to prevent Irish Home Rule, from [Conservative and Unionist Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") leaders such as [Bonar Law](/wiki/Bonar_Law "Bonar Law") and Dublin-based barrister [Edward Carson](/wiki/Edward_Carson "Edward Carson") to militant working class unionists in Ireland. This sparked the [Home Rule Crisis](/wiki/Home_Rule_Crisis "Home Rule Crisis"). In September 1912, more than 500,000 unionists signed the [Ulster Covenant](/wiki/Ulster_Covenant "Ulster Covenant"), pledging to oppose Home Rule by any means and to defy any Irish government.[[49]](#cite_note-53) In 1914, unionists [smuggled thousands of rifles and rounds of ammunition](/wiki/Larne_gun-running "Larne gun-running") from [Imperial Germany](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") for use by the [Ulster Volunteers](/wiki/Ulster_Volunteers "Ulster Volunteers") (UVF), a paramilitary organisation formed to oppose Home Rule. Irish nationalists had also formed a paramilitary organisation, the [Irish Volunteers](/wiki/Irish_Volunteers "Irish Volunteers"). It sought to ensure Home Rule was implemented, and it [smuggled its own weapons into Ireland](/wiki/Howth_gun-running "Howth gun-running") a few months after the Ulster Volunteers.[[50]](#cite_note-54) Ireland seemed to be on the brink of civil war.[[51]](#cite_note-55)

Unionists were in a minority in Ireland as a whole, but a majority in the province of [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster"), especially the counties [Antrim](/wiki/County_Antrim "County Antrim"), [Down](/wiki/County_Down "County Down"), [Armagh](/wiki/County_Armagh "County Armagh") and [Londonderry](/wiki/County_Londonderry "County Londonderry").[[52]](#cite_note-StephenGwynn-56) Unionists argued that if Home Rule could not be stopped then all or part of Ulster should be excluded from it.[[53]](#cite_note-57) In May 1914, the UK Government introduced an Amending Bill to allow for 'Ulster' to be excluded from Home Rule. There was then debate over how much of Ulster should be excluded and for how long. Some Ulster unionists were willing to tolerate the 'loss' of some mainly-Catholic areas of the province.[[54]](#cite_note-58) The crisis was interrupted by the outbreak of the [First World War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") in August 1914, and [Ireland's involvement in it](/wiki/Ireland_and_World_War_I "Ireland and World War I"). The UK government abandoned the Amending Bill, and instead rushed through a new bill, the [Suspensory Act 1914](/wiki/Suspensory_Act_1914 "Suspensory Act 1914"), suspending Home Rule for the duration of the war,[[55]](#cite_note-59) with the exclusion of Ulster still to be decided.[[56]](#cite_note-60)

### Partition of Ireland

Main article: [Partition of Ireland](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election_%28Ireland%29_map_-_winning_party_vote_share_by_constituency.svg/250px-1918_United_Kingdom_general_election_%28Ireland%29_map_-_winning_party_vote_share_by_constituency.svg.png)](/wiki/File:1918_United_Kingdom_general_election_(Ireland)_map_-_winning_party_vote_share_by_constituency.svg)

Result of the [1918 general election in Ireland](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election_in_Ireland "1918 United Kingdom general election in Ireland")

By the end of the war (during which the 1916 [Easter Rising](/wiki/Easter_Rising "Easter Rising") had taken place), most Irish nationalists now wanted full independence rather than home rule. In September 1919, British Prime Minister [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George") tasked a committee with planning another home rule bill. Headed by [English unionist](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland") politician [Walter Long](/wiki/Walter_Long,_1st_Viscount_Long "Walter Long, 1st Viscount Long"), it was known as the 'Long Committee'. It decided that two devolved governments should be established—one for the nine counties of Ulster and one for the rest of Ireland—together with a [Council of Ireland](/wiki/Council_of_Ireland "Council of Ireland") for the "encouragement of Irish unity".[[57]](#cite_note-61) Most Ulster unionists wanted the territory of the Ulster government to be reduced to six counties so that it would have a larger Protestant unionist majority, which they believed would guarantee its longevity. The six counties of [Antrim](/wiki/County_Antrim "County Antrim"), [Down](/wiki/County_Down "County Down"), [Armagh](/wiki/County_Armagh "County Armagh"), [Londonderry](/wiki/County_Londonderry "County Londonderry"), [Tyrone](/wiki/County_Tyrone "County Tyrone") and [Fermanagh](/wiki/County_Fermanagh "County Fermanagh") comprised the maximum area unionists believed they could dominate.[[58]](#cite_note-62) The area that was to become Northern Ireland included counties Fermanagh and Tyrone, even though they had nationalist majorities in the [1918 Irish general election](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election_in_Ireland "1918 United Kingdom general election in Ireland").[[59]](#cite_note-63)

Events overtook the government. In the 1918 Irish general election, the pro-independence [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin") party won the overwhelming majority of Irish seats. Sinn Féin's elected members boycotted the British parliament and founded a separate Irish parliament ([Dáil Éireann](/wiki/First_D%C3%A1il "First Dáil")), [declaring an independent Irish Republic](/wiki/Irish_Declaration_of_Independence "Irish Declaration of Independence") covering the whole island. Many [Irish republicans](/wiki/Irish_republicans "Irish republicans") blamed the British establishment for the sectarian divisions in Ireland, and believed that Ulster unionism would fade once British rule was ended.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynch201951–52-64) The British authorities outlawed the Dáil in September 1919,[[61]](#cite_note-65) and a guerrilla conflict developed as the [Irish Republican Army](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Army_(1919%E2%80%931922) "Irish Republican Army (1919–1922)") (IRA) began attacking British forces. This became known as the [Irish War of Independence](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence").[[62]](#cite_note-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Ulster_Welcomes_Her_King_%26_Queen_%2810990906846%29.jpg/250px-Ulster_Welcomes_Her_King_%26_Queen_%2810990906846%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ulster_Welcomes_Her_King_%26_Queen_(10990906846).jpg)

Crowds in Belfast for the state opening of the Northern Ireland Parliament on 22 June 1921

Meanwhile, the [Government of Ireland Act 1920](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920 "Government of Ireland Act 1920") passed through the British parliament in 1920. It would divide Ireland into two self-governing UK territories: the six northeastern counties (Northern Ireland) being ruled from [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast"), and the other twenty-six counties ([Southern Ireland](/wiki/Southern_Ireland_(1921%E2%80%931922) "Southern Ireland (1921–1922)")) being ruled from [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"). Both would have a shared [Lord Lieutenant of Ireland](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_Ireland "Lord Lieutenant of Ireland"), who would appoint both governments and a [Council of Ireland](/wiki/Council_of_Ireland "Council of Ireland"), which the UK government intended to evolve into an all-Ireland parliament.[[63]](#cite_note-67) The Act received [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") that December, becoming the Government of Ireland Act 1920. It came into force on 3 May 1921,[[64]](#cite_note-O'Day_299-68)[[65]](#cite_note-69) [partitioning Ireland](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland") and creating Northern Ireland. the [1921 Irish elections](/wiki/1921_Northern_Ireland_and_Southern_Ireland_general_elections "1921 Northern Ireland and Southern Ireland general elections") were held on 24 May, in which unionists won most seats in the Northern Ireland parliament. It first met on 7 June and formed its [first devolved government](/wiki/Craigavon_ministry "Craigavon ministry"), headed by Ulster Unionist Party leader [James Craig](/wiki/James_Craig,_1st_Viscount_Craigavon "James Craig, 1st Viscount Craigavon"). Irish nationalist members refused to attend. King George V addressed the ceremonial opening of the Northern parliament on 22 June.[[64]](#cite_note-O'Day_299-68)

During 1920–22, in what became Northern Ireland, partition was accompanied by violence "in defence or opposition to the new settlement"[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynch201911,_100–101-25) during [The Troubles in Ulster (1920–1922)](/wiki/The_Troubles_in_Ulster_(1920%E2%80%931922) "The Troubles in Ulster (1920–1922)"). The IRA carried out attacks on British forces in the north-east but was less active than in the rest of Ireland. Protestant loyalists attacked Catholics in reprisal for IRA actions. In the summer of 1920, sectarian violence erupted in Belfast and Derry, and there were mass burnings of Catholic property in [Lisburn](/wiki/Lisburn "Lisburn") and [Banbridge](/wiki/Banbridge "Banbridge").[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynch201990–92-70) Conflict continued intermittently for two years, mostly in [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast"), which saw "savage and unprecedented" [communal violence](/wiki/Communal_violence "Communal violence") between Protestants and Catholics, including rioting, gun battles, and bombings. Homes, businesses, and churches were attacked and people were expelled from workplaces and mixed neighbourhoods.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynch201911,_100–101-25) More than 500 were killed[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynch201999-26) and more than 10,000 became refugees, most of them Catholics.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynch201971–76-71) The [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") was deployed and the [Ulster Special Constabulary](/wiki/Ulster_Special_Constabulary "Ulster Special Constabulary") (USC) was formed to help the regular police. The USC was almost wholly Protestant. Members of the USC and regular police were involved in reprisal attacks on Catholic civilians.[[68]](#cite_note-72) A truce between British forces and the IRA was established on 11 July 1921, ending the fighting in most of Ireland. However, communal violence continued in Belfast, and in 1922 the IRA launched a guerrilla offensive along the new [Irish border](/wiki/Irish_border "Irish border").[[69]](#cite_note-73)

The [Anglo-Irish Treaty](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Treaty "Anglo-Irish Treaty") was signed between representatives of the governments of the UK and the [Irish Republic](/wiki/Irish_Republic "Irish Republic") on 6 December 1921, laying out the process for the creation of the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State"). Under the terms of the treaty, Northern Ireland would become part of the Free State unless its government opted out by presenting an address to the king, although in practice partition remained in place.[[70]](#cite_note-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Coat_of_Arms_of_Northern_Ireland.svg/250px-Coat_of_Arms_of_Northern_Ireland.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_Arms_of_Northern_Ireland.svg)

The [Coat of arms of the Government of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Northern_Ireland "Coat of arms of Northern Ireland") used between 1924 and 1973

The Irish Free State came into existence on 6 December 1922, and on the following day, the [Parliament of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Northern_Ireland "Parliament of Northern Ireland") resolved to exercise its right to opt out of the Free State by making an address to King [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V").[[71]](#cite_note-75) The text of the address was:

> Most Gracious Sovereign, We, your Majesty's most dutiful and loyal subjects, the Senators and Commons of Northern Ireland in Parliament assembled, having learnt of the passing of the [Irish Free State Constitution Act 1922](/wiki/Irish_Free_State_Constitution_Act_1922 "Irish Free State Constitution Act 1922"), being the Act of Parliament for the ratification of the Articles of Agreement for a Treaty between Great Britain and Ireland, do, by this humble Address, pray your Majesty that the powers of the Parliament and Government of the Irish Free State shall no longer extend to Northern Ireland.[[72]](#cite_note-76)

Shortly afterwards, the [Irish Boundary Commission](/wiki/Irish_Boundary_Commission "Irish Boundary Commission") was established to decide on the border between the Irish Free State and Northern Ireland. Owing to the outbreak of the [Irish Civil War](/wiki/Irish_Civil_War "Irish Civil War"), the work of the commission was delayed until 1925. The Free State government and Irish nationalists hoped for a large transfer of territory to the Free State, as many border areas had nationalist majorities. Many believed this would leave the remaining Northern Ireland territory too small to be viable.[[73]](#cite_note-77) However, the commission's final report recommended only small transfers of territory, and in both directions. The Free State, Northern Ireland, and UK governments agreed to suppress the report and accept the *status quo*, while the UK government agreed that the Free State would no longer have to pay a share of the UK national debt.[[74]](#cite_note-78)

### Established government and boundaries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Northern_Ireland_Cabinet_1921.jpg/250px-Northern_Ireland_Cabinet_1921.jpg)](/wiki/File:Northern_Ireland_Cabinet_1921.jpg)

James Craig (centre) with members of the first government of Northern Ireland

Northern Ireland's border was drawn to give it "a decisive Protestant majority". At the time of its creation, Northern Ireland's population was two-thirds Protestant and one-third Catholic.[[18]](#cite_note-McKittrick-McVea_p5-22) Most Protestants were unionists/loyalists who sought to maintain Northern Ireland as a part of the United Kingdom, while most Catholics were Irish nationalists/republicans who sought an independent [United Ireland](/wiki/United_Ireland "United Ireland"). There was mutual self-imposed [segregation in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Segregation_in_Northern_Ireland "Segregation in Northern Ireland") between Protestants and Catholics such as in education, housing, and often employment.[[75]](#cite_note-Making_Sense_17-19-79)

For its first fifty years, Northern Ireland had an unbroken series of [Ulster Unionist Party governments](/wiki/Government_of_Northern_Ireland_(1921%E2%80%931972) "Government of Northern Ireland (1921–1972)").[[76]](#cite_note-80) Every prime minister and almost every minister of these governments were members of the [Orange Order](/wiki/Orange_Order "Orange Order"), as were all but 11 of the 149 [Ulster Unionist Party](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party") (UUP) MPs elected during this time.[[77]](#cite_note-81) Almost all judges and magistrates were Protestant, many of them closely associated with the UUP. Northern Ireland's new police force was the [Royal Ulster Constabulary](/wiki/Royal_Ulster_Constabulary "Royal Ulster Constabulary") (RUC), which succeeded the [Royal Irish Constabulary](/wiki/Royal_Irish_Constabulary "Royal Irish Constabulary") (RIC). It too was almost wholly Protestant and lacked operational independence, responding to directions from government ministers. The RUC and the reserve [Ulster Special Constabulary](/wiki/Ulster_Special_Constabulary "Ulster Special Constabulary") (USC) were militarized police forces due to the perceived threat of militant republicanism. In 1936 the British advocacy group - the [National Council for Civil Liberties](/wiki/Liberty_(advocacy_group) "Liberty (advocacy group)") characterised the USC as "nothing but the organised army of the Unionist party".[[78]](#cite_note-82) They "had at their disposal the [Special Powers Act](/wiki/Civil_Authorities_(Special_Powers)_Act_(Northern_Ireland)_1922 "Civil Authorities (Special Powers) Act (Northern Ireland) 1922"), a sweeping piece of legislation which allowed arrests without warrant, internment without trial, unlimited search powers, and bans on meetings and publications".[[79]](#cite_note-83) This 1922 Act was made permanent in 1933 and was not repealed until 1973.[[80]](#cite_note-84)

The [Nationalist Party](/wiki/Nationalist_Party_(Northern_Ireland) "Nationalist Party (Northern Ireland)") was the main political party in opposition to the UUP governments. However, its elected members often protested by [abstaining](/wiki/Abstentionism "Abstentionism") from the Northern Ireland parliament, and many nationalists did not vote in parliamentary elections.[[75]](#cite_note-Making_Sense_17-19-79) Other early nationalist groups which campaigned against partition included the [National League of the North](/wiki/National_League_of_the_North "National League of the North") (formed in 1928), the [Northern Council for Unity](/wiki/Northern_Council_for_Unity "Northern Council for Unity") (formed in 1937) and the [Irish Anti-Partition League](/wiki/Irish_Anti-Partition_League "Irish Anti-Partition League") (formed in 1945).[[81]](#cite_note-85)

The Local Government Act (Northern Ireland) of 1922 allowed for the altering of municipal and rural boundaries. This Act led to the [gerrymandering](/wiki/Gerrymandering "Gerrymandering") of local election boundaries in the Nationalists majority cities of Derry City, Enniskillen, Omagh, Armagh and many other towns and rural districts. That action ensured Unionist control over local councils in areas where they were a minority.[[82]](#cite_note-86) The UUP governments, and some UUP-dominated local authorities, discriminated against the Catholic and Irish nationalist minority; especially by the gerrymandering of local electoral boundaries, the allocation of public housing, public sector employment, and policing, showing "a consistent and irrefutable pattern of deliberate discrimination against Catholics".[[83]](#cite_note-87) Many Catholics/Nationalists saw the gerrymandered local electoral boundaries and the abolishing of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") as proof of government-sponsored discrimination. Until 1969 a system was in place called [plural voting](/wiki/Plural_voting "Plural voting") which was a practice whereby one person might be able to vote multiple times in an election. Property and business owners could vote both in the constituency where their property lay and that in which they lived, if the two were different. This system often resulted in one person being able to cast multiple votes.[[84]](#cite_note-88) Decades later, UUP [First Minister of Northern Ireland](/wiki/First_Minister_and_deputy_First_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland "First Minister and deputy First Minister of Northern Ireland"), [David Trimble](/wiki/David_Trimble "David Trimble"), said that Northern Ireland under the UUP had been a "cold house" for Catholics.[[85]](#cite_note-89)

### Offer of Irish unity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Opening_of_the_new_Northern_Ireland_Parliament_Buildings.jpg/250px-Opening_of_the_new_Northern_Ireland_Parliament_Buildings.jpg)](/wiki/File:Opening_of_the_new_Northern_Ireland_Parliament_Buildings.jpg)

Opening of the Northern Ireland parliament buildings ([Stormont](/wiki/Parliament_Buildings_(Northern_Ireland) "Parliament Buildings (Northern Ireland)")) in 1932

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), recruitment to the British military was noticeably lower than the high levels reached during World War I. In June 1940, to encourage the [neutral Irish state](/wiki/Irish_neutrality "Irish neutrality") to join with the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II"), British Prime Minister [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill") indicated to Taoiseach [Éamon de Valera](/wiki/%C3%89amon_de_Valera "Éamon de Valera") that the British government would encourage Irish unity, but believing that Churchill could not deliver, de Valera declined the offer.[[86]](#cite_note-90) The British did not inform the government of Northern Ireland that they had made the offer to the Dublin government, and de Valera's rejection was not publicised until 1970. Belfast was a key industrial city in the UK's war effort, producing ships, tanks, aircraft, and munitions. The unemployment that had been so persistent in the 1930s disappeared, and labour shortages appeared, prompting migration from the Free State. The city was thinly defended, and had only 24 anti-aircraft guns. Richard [Dawson Bates](/wiki/Dawson_Bates "Dawson Bates"), the Minister for Home Affairs, had prepared too late, assuming that Belfast was far enough away to be safe. The city's fire brigade was inadequate, and as the Northern Ireland government had been reluctant to spend money on air raid shelters, it only started to build them after [the Blitz](/wiki/The_Blitz "The Blitz") in London during the autumn of 1940. There were no searchlights in the city, which made shooting down enemy bombers more difficult. In April–May 1941, the [Belfast Blitz](/wiki/Belfast_Blitz "Belfast Blitz") began when the *[Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe")* launched a series of raids that were the most deadly seen outside London. Working-class areas in the north and east of the city were particularly hard hit, and over 1,000 people were killed and hundreds were seriously injured. Tens of thousands of people fled the city in fear of future attacks. In the final raid, *Luftwaffe* bombs inflicted extensive damage to the docks and the [Harland & Wolff](/wiki/Harland_%26_Wolff "Harland & Wolff") shipyard, closing it for six months. Half of the city's houses had been destroyed, highlighting the terrible slum conditions in Belfast, and about £20 million worth of damage was caused. The Northern Ireland government was criticised heavily for its lack of preparation, and Northern Ireland Prime Minister [J. M. Andrews](/wiki/J._M._Andrews "J. M. Andrews") resigned. There was a major munitions strike in 1944.[[87]](#cite_note-91)

The [Ireland Act 1949](/wiki/Ireland_Act_1949 "Ireland Act 1949") gave the first legal guarantee that the region would not cease to be part of the United Kingdom without the consent of the [Parliament of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Northern_Ireland "Parliament of Northern Ireland"). From 1956 to 1962, the [Irish Republican Army](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Army_(1922%E2%80%931969) "Irish Republican Army (1922–1969)") (IRA) carried out a limited guerrilla campaign in border areas of Northern Ireland, called the [Border Campaign](/wiki/Border_campaign_(Irish_Republican_Army) "Border campaign (Irish Republican Army)"). It aimed to destabilize Northern Ireland and bring about an end to partition but failed.[[88]](#cite_note-92) In 1965, Northern Ireland's Prime Minister [Terence O'Neill](/wiki/Terence_O%27Neill "Terence O'Neill") met the Taoiseach, [Seán Lemass](/wiki/Se%C3%A1n_Lemass "Seán Lemass"). It was the first meeting between the two heads of government since partition.[[89]](#cite_note-93)

### The Troubles

Main article: [The Troubles](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Troubles_deaths_by_perpetrator.png/500px-Troubles_deaths_by_perpetrator.png)](/wiki/File:Troubles_deaths_by_perpetrator.png)

Responsibility for Troubles-related deaths between 1969 and 2001

The Troubles, which started in the late 1960s, consisted of about 30 years of recurring acts of intense violence during which 3,254 people were killed[[90]](#cite_note-94) with over 50,000 casualties.[[91]](#cite_note-95) From 1969 to 2003 there were over 36,900 shooting incidents and over 16,200 bombings or attempted bombings associated with The Troubles.[[29]](#cite_note-auto2-33) The conflict was caused by escalating tensions between the [Irish nationalist](/wiki/Irish_nationalist "Irish nationalist") minority and the dominant [unionist majority](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland"); Irish nationalists object to Northern Ireland staying within the United Kingdom.[[92]](#cite_note-96) From 1967 to 1972 the [Northern Ireland Civil Rights Association](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Civil_Rights_Association "Northern Ireland Civil Rights Association") (NICRA), which modelled itself on the US civil rights movement, led a campaign of [civil resistance](/wiki/Civil_resistance "Civil resistance") to anti-Catholic discrimination in housing, employment, policing, and electoral procedures. The franchise for local government elections included only rate-payers and their spouses, and so excluded over a quarter of the electorate. While the majority of disenfranchised electors were Protestant, Catholics were over-represented since they were poorer and had more adults still living in the family home.[[93]](#cite_note-97)

NICRA's campaign, seen by many unionists as an [Irish republican](/wiki/Irish_republican "Irish republican") front, and the violent reaction to it proved to be a precursor to a more violent period.[[94]](#cite_note-98) As early as 1969, armed campaigns of paramilitary groups began, including the [Provisional IRA campaign of 1969–1997](/wiki/Provisional_Irish_Republican_Army_campaign "Provisional Irish Republican Army campaign") which was aimed at the end of British rule in Northern Ireland and the creation of a [United Ireland](/wiki/United_Ireland "United Ireland"), and the [Ulster Volunteer Force](/wiki/Ulster_Volunteer_Force "Ulster Volunteer Force"), formed in 1966 in response to the perceived erosion of both the British character and unionist domination of Northern Ireland. The state security forces – the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") and the police (the [Royal Ulster Constabulary](/wiki/Royal_Ulster_Constabulary "Royal Ulster Constabulary")) – were also involved in the violence. The UK Government's position is that its forces were neutral in the conflict, trying to uphold law and order in Northern Ireland and the right of the people of Northern Ireland to democratic self-determination. Republicans regarded the state forces as [combatants](/wiki/Combatant "Combatant") in the conflict, pointing to the [collusion between the state forces and the loyalist paramilitaries](/wiki/The_Troubles#Collusion_between_security_forces_and_paramilitaries "The Troubles") as proof of this. The "Ballast" investigation by the [Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland](/wiki/Police_Ombudsman_for_Northern_Ireland "Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland") has confirmed that British forces, and in particular the RUC, did collude with loyalist paramilitaries, were involved in murder, and did obstruct the course of justice when such claims had been investigated,[[95]](#cite_note-Ballast-99) although the extent to which such collusion occurred is still disputed.

As a consequence of the worsening security situation, the autonomous regional government for Northern Ireland was suspended in 1972. Alongside the violence, there was a political deadlock between the major political parties in Northern Ireland, including those who condemned the violence, over the future status of Northern Ireland and the form of government there should be within Northern Ireland. In 1973, [Northern Ireland held a referendum](/wiki/1973_Northern_Ireland_border_poll "1973 Northern Ireland border poll") to determine if it should remain in the United Kingdom, or be part of a united Ireland. The vote went heavily in favour (98.9%) of maintaining the status quo. Approximately 57.5% of the total electorate voted in support, but only 1% of Catholics voted following a boycott organised by the [Social Democratic and Labour Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_and_Labour_Party "Social Democratic and Labour Party") (SDLP).[[96]](#cite_note-100) The deaths of 10 men during the [1981 Irish hunger strike](/wiki/1981_Irish_hunger_strike "1981 Irish hunger strike") brought worldwide attention to the Republican prisoners being held in prison ([HM Prison Maze](/wiki/HM_Prison_Maze "HM Prison Maze")) in Northern Ireland.[[97]](#cite_note-101)

### Peace process

Main article: [Northern Ireland peace process](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_peace_process "Northern Ireland peace process")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Foyle_Bridge_Derry_at_Dusk_Oblique.jpg/250px-Foyle_Bridge_Derry_at_Dusk_Oblique.jpg)](/wiki/File:Foyle_Bridge_Derry_at_Dusk_Oblique.jpg)

[Derry Peace Bridge](/wiki/Peace_Bridge_(Foyle) "Peace Bridge (Foyle)"), over the [River Foyle](/wiki/River_Foyle "River Foyle")

The Troubles were brought to an uneasy end by a peace process that included the declaration of ceasefires by most paramilitary organisations and the complete decommissioning of their weapons, the reform of the police, and the corresponding withdrawal of army troops from the streets and sensitive border areas such as South Armagh and [Fermanagh](/wiki/Fermanagh "Fermanagh"), as agreed by the signatories to the [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement") (also known as the "Belfast Agreement"). This reiterated the long-held British position, which had never before been fully acknowledged by successive Irish governments, that Northern Ireland will remain within the United Kingdom until a majority of voters in Northern Ireland decides otherwise. The [Constitution of Ireland](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ireland "Constitution of Ireland") was amended in 1999 to remove a claim of the "Irish nation" to sovereignty over the entire island (in Article 2).[[98]](#cite_note-102)

The new [Articles 2 and 3](/wiki/Articles_2_and_3_of_the_Constitution_of_Ireland "Articles 2 and 3 of the Constitution of Ireland"), added to the Constitution to replace the earlier articles, implicitly acknowledge that the status of Northern Ireland, and its relationships within the rest of the United Kingdom and with the Republic of Ireland, would only be changed with the agreement of a majority of voters in each jurisdiction. This aspect was also central to the Belfast Agreement which was signed in 1998 and ratified by referendums held simultaneously in both Northern Ireland and the Republic. At the same time, the UK Government recognised for the first time, as part of the prospective, the so-called "Irish dimension": the principle that the people of the island of Ireland as a whole have the right, without any outside interference, to solve the issues between North and South by mutual consent.[[99]](#cite_note-Parliament-103) The latter statement was key to winning support for the agreement from nationalists. It established a devolved power-sharing government, the [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly"), located on the [Stormont Estate](/wiki/Stormont_Estate "Stormont Estate"), which must consist of both unionist and nationalist parties. These institutions were suspended by the [UK Government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") in 2002 after [Police Service of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Police_Service_of_Northern_Ireland "Police Service of Northern Ireland") (PSNI) allegations of spying by people working for Sinn Féin at the Assembly ([Stormontgate](/wiki/Stormontgate "Stormontgate")). The resulting case against the accused Sinn Féin member collapsed.[[100]](#cite_note-104)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Scottish_and_Northern_Ireland_Ministers.jpg/250px-Scottish_and_Northern_Ireland_Ministers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scottish_and_Northern_Ireland_Ministers.jpg)

[First Minister](/wiki/First_Minister_and_deputy_First_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland "First Minister and deputy First Minister of Northern Ireland") [Ian Paisley](/wiki/Ian_Paisley "Ian Paisley") and deputy First Minister [Martin McGuinness](/wiki/Martin_McGuinness "Martin McGuinness"), signs the Joint Agreement with [First Minister of Scotland](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland"), [Alex Salmond](/wiki/Alex_Salmond "Alex Salmond"), May 2008

On 28 July 2005, the Provisional IRA declared an end to its campaign and has since decommissioned what is thought to be all of its [arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal "Arsenal"). This final act of decommissioning was performed under the watch of the [Independent International Commission on Decommissioning](/wiki/Independent_International_Commission_on_Decommissioning "Independent International Commission on Decommissioning") (IICD) and two external church witnesses. Many unionists, however, remained sceptical. The IICD later confirmed that the main loyalist paramilitary groups, the [Ulster Defence Association](/wiki/Ulster_Defence_Association "Ulster Defence Association"), UVF, and the [Red Hand Commando](/wiki/Red_Hand_Commando "Red Hand Commando"), had decommissioned what is thought to be all of their arsenals, witnessed by former archbishop [Robin Eames](/wiki/Robin_Eames "Robin Eames") and a former top civil servant.[[101]](#cite_note-105)

Politicians elected to the Assembly at the [2003 Assembly election](/wiki/2003_Northern_Ireland_Assembly_election "2003 Northern Ireland Assembly election") were called together on 15 May 2006 under the Northern Ireland Act 2006[[102]](#cite_note-106) to elect a [First Minister and deputy First Minister of Northern Ireland](/wiki/First_Minister_and_deputy_First_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland "First Minister and deputy First Minister of Northern Ireland") and choose the members of an Executive (before 25 November 2006) as a preliminary step to the restoration of devolved government.

Following the [election on 7 March 2007](/wiki/2007_Northern_Ireland_Assembly_election "2007 Northern Ireland Assembly election"), the devolved government returned on 8 May 2007 with [Democratic Unionist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party") (DUP) leader [Ian Paisley](/wiki/Ian_Paisley "Ian Paisley") and Sinn Féin deputy leader [Martin McGuinness](/wiki/Martin_McGuinness "Martin McGuinness") taking office as First Minister and deputy First Minister, respectively.[[103]](#cite_note-107) In its [white paper](/wiki/White_paper "White paper") on [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") the United Kingdom government reiterated its commitment to the Belfast Agreement. Concerning Northern Ireland's status, it said that the UK Government's "clearly-stated preference is to retain Northern Ireland's current constitutional position: as part of the UK, but with strong links to Ireland".[[104]](#cite_note-108)

### Executive crisis 2022–2024

Further information: [2024 Northern Ireland Executive formation](/wiki/2024_Northern_Ireland_Executive_formation "2024 Northern Ireland Executive formation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/First_Minister_Humza_Yousaf_meets_with_First_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland_designate_Michelle_O%27Neill%2C_2023.jpg/250px-First_Minister_Humza_Yousaf_meets_with_First_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland_designate_Michelle_O%27Neill%2C_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_Minister_Humza_Yousaf_meets_with_First_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland_designate_Michelle_O%27Neill,_2023.jpg)

O'Neill, as First Minister–designate, meets with [First Minister of Scotland](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland"), [Humza Yousaf](/wiki/Humza_Yousaf "Humza Yousaf"), 2022

On 3 February 2022, [Paul Givan](/wiki/Paul_Givan "Paul Givan") resigned as first minister, which automatically resigned [Michelle O'Neill](/wiki/Michelle_O%27Neill "Michelle O'Neill") as deputy first minister and collapsed the executive of Northern Ireland.[[105]](#cite_note-109) Following the [2022 Assembly election](/wiki/2022_Northern_Ireland_Assembly_election "2022 Northern Ireland Assembly election"), [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin") won the largest number of seats with 27 seats, becoming the largest political party in the [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly").[[106]](#cite_note-110) Their unionist counterparts, the [Democratic Unionist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party") (DUP) came second with 25 seats.[[107]](#cite_note-111) As a result of being the largest party, this put O'Neill in line to become the First Minister of Northern Ireland and the DUP leader to become the deputy First Minister of Northern Ireland.[[108]](#cite_note-112) However, O'Neill did not take up the position until February 2024 because, as part of its opposition to the [Northern Ireland Protocol](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Protocol "Northern Ireland Protocol"), the DUP refused to nominate a deputy First minister and there was therefore no functioning executive of Northern Ireland.[[109]](#cite_note-O'Neill,_Michelle-113)

On 30 January 2024, leader of the DUP [Jeffrey Donaldson](/wiki/Jeffrey_Donaldson "Jeffrey Donaldson") announced that the DUP would restore an executive government on the condition that new legislation was passed by the UK House of Commons.[[110]](#cite_note-114) This paved the way for O'Neill, as nationalist leader to be sworn in as [First Minister of Northern Ireland](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland "First Minister of Northern Ireland").[[111]](#cite_note-115)[[112]](#cite_note-116)[[113]](#cite_note-117) O'Neill assumed office on 3 February 2024, becoming the first ever Irish nationalist, republican or Catholic to hold that position. In her [Stormont](/wiki/Stormont_Estate "Stormont Estate") acceptance speech, she again broke with republican tradition, by using the term, Northern Ireland. She pledged in her speech to represent all and to show respect to the [royal family](/wiki/British_royal_family "British royal family").[[114]](#cite_note-:3-118)[[109]](#cite_note-O'Neill,_Michelle-113)[[115]](#cite_note-119)[[116]](#cite_note-120) On 5 February, O'Neill held meetings with [Rishi Sunak](/wiki/Rishi_Sunak "Rishi Sunak"), [Chris Heaton-Harris](/wiki/Chris_Heaton-Harris "Chris Heaton-Harris"), [Leo Varadkar](/wiki/Leo_Varadkar "Leo Varadkar") and ministers of the [Northern Ireland Executive](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Executive "Northern Ireland Executive"). High on the agenda is the request for additional financial support for the Northern Ireland government in excess of the £3.3 billion package already pledged from the [HM Treasury](/wiki/HM_Treasury "HM Treasury").[[117]](#cite_note-121)[[118]](#cite_note-122)

In November 2024, O'Neill became the first senior Sinn Féin figure to take part in an official [Remembrance Sunday](/wiki/Remembrance_Sunday "Remembrance Sunday") ceremony, laying a laurel wreath at the [Belfast Cenotaph](/wiki/Belfast_Cenotaph "Belfast Cenotaph") at [City Hall](/wiki/Belfast_City_Hall "Belfast City Hall").[[119]](#cite_note-123) A banner was subsequently hung outside her office brandishing O'Neill a "traitor"; police investigated the incident as "hate-motivated".[[120]](#cite_note-124)

## Politics

Main article: [Politics of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Politics_of_Northern_Ireland "Politics of Northern Ireland")

### Background

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Political_Parties_of_Northern_Ireland.png/500px-Political_Parties_of_Northern_Ireland.png)](/wiki/File:Political_Parties_of_Northern_Ireland.png)

A flowchart illustrating all the political parties that have existed throughout the history of Northern Ireland and leading up to its formation (covering 1889 to 2020)

The main political divide in Northern Ireland is between unionists, who wish to see Northern Ireland continue as part of the United Kingdom, and nationalists, who wish to see Northern Ireland unified with the Republic of Ireland, independent from the United Kingdom. These two opposing views are linked to deeper cultural divisions. Unionists are predominantly [Ulster Protestant](/wiki/Ulster_Protestant "Ulster Protestant"), descendants of mainly [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people"), English, and [Huguenot](/wiki/Huguenot "Huguenot") settlers as well as [Gaels](/wiki/Gaels "Gaels") who converted to one of the Protestant denominations. Nationalists are overwhelmingly Catholic and descend from the population predating the settlement, with a minority from the [Scottish Highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands") as well as some converts from Protestantism. Discrimination against nationalists under the [Stormont](/wiki/Parliament_Buildings_(Northern_Ireland) "Parliament Buildings (Northern Ireland)") government (1921–1972) gave rise to the [civil rights movement](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_civil_rights_movement "Northern Ireland civil rights movement") in the 1960s.[[121]](#cite_note-125)

While some unionists argue that discrimination was not just due to religious or political bigotry, but also the result of more complex socio-economic, socio-political and geographical factors,[[122]](#cite_note-126) its existence, and the manner in which nationalist anger at it was handled, were a major contributing factor to the Troubles. The political unrest went through its most violent phase between 1968 and 1994.[[123]](#cite_note-127)

In 2007, 36% of the population defined themselves as unionist, 24% as nationalist, and 40% defined themselves as neither.[[124]](#cite_note-128) According to a 2015 opinion poll, 70% express a long-term preference of the maintenance of Northern Ireland's membership of the United Kingdom (either [directly ruled](/wiki/Direct_rule_over_Northern_Ireland "Direct rule over Northern Ireland") or with [devolved government](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution")), while 14% express a preference for membership of a united Ireland.[[125]](#cite_note-NILT2015-129) This discrepancy can be explained by the overwhelming preference among Protestants to remain a part of the UK (93%), while Catholic preferences are spread across several solutions to the constitutional question including remaining a part of the UK (47%), a united Ireland (32%), Northern Ireland becoming an independent state (4%), and those who "don't know" (16%).[[125]](#cite_note-NILT2015-129)

Official voting figures, which reflect views on the "national question" along with issues of the candidate, geography, personal loyalty, and historic voting patterns, show 54% of Northern Ireland voters vote for unionist parties, 42% vote for nationalist parties, and 4% vote "other". Opinion polls consistently show that the election results are not necessarily an indication of the electorate's stance regarding the constitutional status of Northern Ireland. Most of the population of Northern Ireland is at least nominally Christian, mostly Roman Catholic and Protestant denominations. Many voters (regardless of religious affiliation) are attracted to unionism's [conservative](/wiki/National_conservatism "National conservatism") policies, while other voters are instead attracted to the traditionally leftist Sinn Féin and SDLP and their respective party platforms for [democratic socialism](/wiki/Democratic_socialism "Democratic socialism") and [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy").[[126]](#cite_note-autogenerated2009-130)

For the most part, Protestants feel a strong connection with Great Britain and wish for Northern Ireland to remain part of the United Kingdom. Many Catholics however, generally aspire to a United Ireland or are less certain about how to solve the constitutional question. Catholics have a slight majority in Northern Ireland, according to the latest Northern Ireland census. The make-up of the [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") reflects the appeals of the various parties within the population. Of the 90 [Members of the Legislative Assembly (MLAs)](/wiki/Member_of_the_Legislative_Assembly_(Northern_Ireland) "Member of the Legislative Assembly (Northern Ireland)"), 37 are unionists and 35 are nationalists (the remaining 18 are classified as "other").[[127]](#cite_note-results_maps_charts-131)

The 1998 [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement") acts as a *de facto* [constitution](/wiki/Constitution "Constitution") for Northern Ireland. [Local government in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Local_government_in_Northern_Ireland "Local government in Northern Ireland") since 2015 has been divided between 11 councils with limited responsibilities.[[128]](#cite_note-Devenport-132) The [First Minister and deputy First Minister of Northern Ireland](/wiki/First_Minister_and_deputy_First_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland "First Minister and deputy First Minister of Northern Ireland") are the joint heads of government of Northern Ireland.[[129]](#cite_note-133)[[130]](#cite_note-134)

### Governance

Main articles: [Elections in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Elections_in_Northern_Ireland "Elections in Northern Ireland") and [Law of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Law_of_Northern_Ireland "Law of Northern Ireland")
