[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Parliament_Buildings%2C_Stormont_Estate.png/250px-Parliament_Buildings%2C_Stormont_Estate.png)](/wiki/File:Parliament_Buildings,_Stormont_Estate.png)

[Parliament Buildings](/wiki/Parliament_Buildings_(Northern_Ireland) "Parliament Buildings (Northern Ireland)") at [Stormont](/wiki/Parliament_Buildings_(Northern_Ireland) "Parliament Buildings (Northern Ireland)"), [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast"), seat of the assembly

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/O%E2%80%99Neill_and_Little-Pengelly%2C_March_2024.jpg/250px-O%E2%80%99Neill_and_Little-Pengelly%2C_March_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:O%E2%80%99Neill_and_Little-Pengelly,_March_2024.jpg)

[Michelle O'Neill](/wiki/Michelle_O%27Neill "Michelle O'Neill") (left) and [Emma Little-Pengelly](/wiki/Emma_Little-Pengelly "Emma Little-Pengelly") (right), the incumbent [First Minister and Deputy First Minister of Northern Ireland](/wiki/First_Minister_and_Deputy_First_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland "First Minister and Deputy First Minister of Northern Ireland") respectively (since 2024)

Since 1998, Northern Ireland has had [devolved](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") government within the United Kingdom, presided over by the [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") and a cross-community government (the [Northern Ireland Executive](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Executive "Northern Ireland Executive")).[[131]](#cite_note-135)[[132]](#cite_note-:1-136) The UK Government and UK Parliament are responsible for [reserved and excepted matters](/wiki/Reserved_and_excepted_matters "Reserved and excepted matters"). Reserved matters comprise listed policy areas (such as [civil aviation](/wiki/Civil_aviation "Civil aviation"), [units of measurement](/wiki/Units_of_measurement "Units of measurement"), and [human genetics](/wiki/Human_genetics "Human genetics")) that Parliament may devolve to the Assembly some time in the future. Excepted matters (such as [international relations](/wiki/International_relations "International relations"), taxation and elections) are never expected to be considered for devolution. On all other governmental matters, the Executive together with the 90-member Assembly may legislate for and govern Northern Ireland. Devolution in Northern Ireland is dependent upon participation by members of the Northern Ireland executive in the [North/South Ministerial Council](/wiki/North/South_Ministerial_Council "North/South Ministerial Council"), which coordinates areas of cooperation (such as agriculture, education, and health) between Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland. Additionally, "in recognition of the Irish Government's special interest in Northern Ireland", the [Government of Ireland](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland "Government of Ireland") and [Government of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") co-operate closely on non-devolved matters through the [British–Irish Intergovernmental Conference](/wiki/British%E2%80%93Irish_Intergovernmental_Conference "British–Irish Intergovernmental Conference").

Elections to the [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") are by [single transferable vote](/wiki/Single_transferable_vote "Single transferable vote") with five Members of the Legislative Assembly (MLAs) elected from each of 18 [parliamentary constituencies](/wiki/List_of_parliamentary_constituencies_in_Northern_Ireland "List of parliamentary constituencies in Northern Ireland").[[133]](#cite_note-137) In addition, eighteen representatives (Members of Parliament, MPs) are elected to the [lower house of the UK parliament](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") from the same constituencies using the [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting "First-past-the-post voting") system.[[134]](#cite_note-138) However, not all of those elected take their seats. Sinn Féin MPs, currently seven,[[135]](#cite_note-139) refuse to take the oath to serve the King that is required before MPs are allowed to take their seats.[[136]](#cite_note-140)[[137]](#cite_note-141) In addition, the upper house of the UK parliament, the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), currently has some 25 appointed [members from Northern Ireland](/wiki/List_of_Northern_Ireland_members_of_the_House_of_Lords "List of Northern Ireland members of the House of Lords").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Northern Ireland Office](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Office "Northern Ireland Office") represents the UK Government in Northern Ireland on reserved matters and represents Northern Ireland's interests within the UK Government.[[138]](#cite_note-142) Additionally, the Republic's government also has the right to "put forward views and proposals" on non-devolved matters about Northern Ireland.[[139]](#cite_note-143) The Northern Ireland Office is led by the [Secretary of State for Northern Ireland](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Northern_Ireland "Secretary of State for Northern Ireland"), who sits in the [Cabinet of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom").[[140]](#cite_note-144)[[132]](#cite_note-:1-136)

Northern Ireland is a distinct legal [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction"), separate from the two other jurisdictions in the United Kingdom ([England and Wales](/wiki/English_law "English law"), and [Scotland](/wiki/Scots_law "Scots law")).[[141]](#cite_note-145) Northern Ireland law developed from Irish law that existed before the [partition of Ireland](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland") in 1921. Northern Ireland is a [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") jurisdiction and its common law is similar to that in England and Wales. However, there are important differences in law and procedure between Northern Ireland and England and Wales.[[142]](#cite_note-146) The body of [statute law](/wiki/Statutory_law "Statutory law") affecting Northern Ireland reflects the history of Northern Ireland, including Acts of the Parliament of the United Kingdom, the [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly"), the former [Parliament of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Northern_Ireland "Parliament of Northern Ireland"),[[143]](#cite_note-147)[[132]](#cite_note-:1-136) and the [Parliament of Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland"),[[144]](#cite_note-148)[[145]](#cite_note-149) along with some Acts of the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") and of the [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain") that were extended to Ireland under [Poynings' Law](/wiki/Poynings%27_Law_(confirmation_of_English_statutes) "Poynings' Law (confirmation of English statutes)") between 1494 and 1782.[[146]](#cite_note-150)

### Descriptions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Downhill_Northern_Ireland.jpg/250px-Downhill_Northern_Ireland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Downhill_Northern_Ireland.jpg)

Northern Ireland coast

There is no generally accepted term to describe what Northern Ireland is. It has been described as a country, province, region, and other terms officially, by the press, and in common speech. The choice of term can be controversial and can reveal one's political preferences.[[15]](#cite_note-interpretingNI-19) This has been noted as a problem by several writers on Northern Ireland, with no generally recommended solution.[[14]](#cite_note-alphabeticalNI-18)[[15]](#cite_note-interpretingNI-19)[[16]](#cite_note-placeApart-20)

[ISO 3166-2:GB](/wiki/ISO_3166-2:GB "ISO 3166-2:GB") defines Northern Ireland as a province.[[13]](#cite_note-ISO-17) The UK's submission to the 2007 [United Nations Conference on the Standardization of Geographical Names](/wiki/United_Nations_Conference_on_the_Standardization_of_Geographical_Names "United Nations Conference on the Standardization of Geographical Names") defines the UK as being made up of two countries (England and Scotland), one [principality](/wiki/Principality "Principality") (Wales) and one province (Northern Ireland).[[147]](#cite_note-151) However, this term can be controversial, particularly for nationalists for whom the title province is properly reserved for the traditional province of Ulster, of which Northern Ireland comprises six out of nine counties.[[148]](#cite_note-Guardian_style_guide-152)[[15]](#cite_note-interpretingNI-19)[[149]](#cite_note-dictPolSci-153) Some authors have described the meaning of this term as being equivocal: referring to Northern Ireland as being a province both of the United Kingdom and the traditional country of Ireland.[[150]](#cite_note-unionistPolitics-154)

The UK [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") and the website of the Office of the Prime Minister of the United Kingdom describe the United Kingdom as being made up of four countries, one of these being Northern Ireland.[[12]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-16)[[151]](#cite_note-Number10CountriesWithinACountry-155) Some newspaper style guides also consider country as an acceptable term for Northern Ireland.[[148]](#cite_note-Guardian_style_guide-152) However, some authors reject the term.[[149]](#cite_note-dictPolSci-153)[[14]](#cite_note-alphabeticalNI-18)[[16]](#cite_note-placeApart-20)[[150]](#cite_note-unionistPolitics-154)

"Region" has also been used by UK government agencies[[152]](#cite_note-156) and newspapers.[[148]](#cite_note-Guardian_style_guide-152) Some authors choose this word but note that it is "unsatisfactory".[[15]](#cite_note-interpretingNI-19)[[16]](#cite_note-placeApart-20) Northern Ireland can also be simply described as "part of the UK", including by the UK government offices.[[151]](#cite_note-Number10CountriesWithinACountry-155)

### Alternative names

Main article: [Alternative names for Northern Ireland](/wiki/Alternative_names_for_Northern_Ireland "Alternative names for Northern Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/JAFFE_FOUNTAIN_OUTSIDE_VICTORIA_SQUARE_SHOPPING_CENTRE_-A_FAVOURITE_OF_MINE-_REF-104998_%2817841282323%29.jpg/250px-JAFFE_FOUNTAIN_OUTSIDE_VICTORIA_SQUARE_SHOPPING_CENTRE_-A_FAVOURITE_OF_MINE-_REF-104998_%2817841282323%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:JAFFE_FOUNTAIN_OUTSIDE_VICTORIA_SQUARE_SHOPPING_CENTRE_-A_FAVOURITE_OF_MINE-_REF-104998_(17841282323).jpg)

[Victoria Square Shopping Centre](/wiki/Victoria_Square_Shopping_Centre "Victoria Square Shopping Centre") in Belfast

Many people inside and outside Northern Ireland use other names for Northern Ireland, depending on their point of view. Disagreement on names, and the reading of political symbolism into the use or non-use of a word, also attaches itself to some urban centres. The most notable example is whether Northern Ireland's second-largest city should be called ["Derry" or "Londonderry"](/wiki/Derry-Londonderry_name_dispute "Derry-Londonderry name dispute").

Choice of language and [nomenclature](/wiki/Nomenclature "Nomenclature") in Northern Ireland often reveals the cultural, ethnic, and religious identity of the speaker. Those who do not belong to any group but lean towards one side often tend to use the language of that group. Supporters of unionism in the British media (notably *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")* and the *[Daily Express](/wiki/Daily_Express "Daily Express")*) regularly call Northern Ireland "Ulster".[[153]](#cite_note-157) Many media outlets in the Republic use "North of Ireland" (or simply "the North"),[[154]](#cite_note-Abortion-in-the-North-of-Ireland-158)[[155]](#cite_note-Calls-for-More-Supports-in_Norths-Schools-159)[[156]](#cite_note-RTE-does-not-stop-people-in-the-North-160)[[157]](#cite_note-161)[[158]](#cite_note-162) as well as the "Six Counties".[[159]](#cite_note-163) *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* has also used "the North".[[160]](#cite_note-164)

Government and cultural organisations in Northern Ireland often use the word "Ulster" in their title; for example, the [University of Ulster](/wiki/University_of_Ulster "University of Ulster"), the [Ulster Museum](/wiki/Ulster_Museum "Ulster Museum"), the [Ulster Orchestra](/wiki/Ulster_Orchestra "Ulster Orchestra"), and [BBC Radio Ulster](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Ulster "BBC Radio Ulster").

Although some news bulletins since the 1990s have opted to avoid all contentious terms and use the official name, Northern Ireland, the term "the North" remains commonly used by broadcast media in the Republic.[[154]](#cite_note-Abortion-in-the-North-of-Ireland-158)[[155]](#cite_note-Calls-for-More-Supports-in_Norths-Schools-159)[[156]](#cite_note-RTE-does-not-stop-people-in-the-North-160)

#### Unionist

* **Ulster**, strictly speaking, refers to the province of [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster"), of which six of nine historical counties are in Northern Ireland. The term "Ulster" is widely used by unionists and the British press as shorthand for Northern Ireland, and is also favoured by [Ulster nationalists](/wiki/Ulster_nationalism "Ulster nationalism").[[e]](#cite_note-165) In the past, calls have been made for Northern Ireland's name to be changed to Ulster. This proposal was formally considered by the Government of Northern Ireland in 1937 and by the UK Government in 1949 but no change was made.[[161]](#cite_note-166)
* **The Province** refers to the historic Irish province of Ulster but today is used by some as shorthand for Northern Ireland. The [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), in its editorial guidance for *Reporting the United Kingdom*, states that "the Province" is an appropriate secondary synonym for Northern Ireland, while "Ulster" is not. It also suggests that "people of Northern Ireland" is preferred to "British" or "Irish", and the term "mainland" should be avoided in reporting about Great Britain and Northern Ireland.[[162]](#cite_note-167)

#### Nationalist

* **North of Ireland** – used to avoid using the name given by the British-enacted Government of Ireland Act 1920.
* **The Six Counties** (*na Sé Chontae*) – the Republic of Ireland is similarly described as the Twenty-Six Counties.[[163]](#cite_note-168) Some of the users of these terms contend that using the official name of the region would imply acceptance of the legitimacy of the Government of Ireland Act.
* **The Occupied Six Counties** – used by some republicans.[[164]](#cite_note-169) The Republic, whose legitimacy is similarly not recognised by republicans opposed to the Belfast Agreement, is described as the "Free State", referring to the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State"), which gained independence (as a [Dominion](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion")) in 1922.[[165]](#cite_note-170)
* **British-Occupied Ireland** – Similar in tone to the Occupied Six Counties,[[166]](#cite_note-171) this term is used by more dogmatic republicans, such as [Republican Sinn Féin](/wiki/Republican_Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Republican Sinn Féin"),[[167]](#cite_note-172) who still hold that the [Second Dáil](/wiki/Second_D%C3%A1il "Second Dáil") was the last legitimate government of Ireland and that all governments since have been foreign-imposed usurpations of Irish national self-determination.[[168]](#cite_note-173)

#### Other

* **Norn Iron** or "Norniron" – is an informal and affectionate[[169]](#cite_note-174) local nickname used to refer to Northern Ireland, derived from the pronunciation of the words "Northern Ireland" in an exaggerated Belfast accent. The phrase is seen as a lighthearted way to refer to Northern Ireland, based as it is on regional pronunciation. It often refers to the [Northern Ireland national football team](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_national_football_team "Northern Ireland national football team").[[170]](#cite_note-175)

## Geography and climate

See also: [Geography of Ireland](/wiki/Geography_of_Ireland "Geography of Ireland") and [Geography of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Geography_of_the_United_Kingdom "Geography of the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Causeway-code_poet-4.jpg/250px-Causeway-code_poet-4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Causeway-code_poet-4.jpg)

The [Giant's Causeway](/wiki/Giant%27s_Causeway "Giant's Causeway"), [County Antrim](/wiki/County_Antrim "County Antrim")

The volcanic activity which created the Antrim Plateau also formed the geometric pillars of the [Giant's Causeway](/wiki/Giant%27s_Causeway "Giant's Causeway") on the north Antrim coast. Also in north Antrim are the [Carrick-a-Rede Rope Bridge](/wiki/Carrick-a-Rede_Rope_Bridge "Carrick-a-Rede Rope Bridge"), [Mussenden Temple](/wiki/Mussenden_Temple "Mussenden Temple") and the [Glens of Antrim](/wiki/Glens_of_Antrim "Glens of Antrim"). Northern Ireland was covered by an [ice sheet](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet") for most of the [last ice age](/wiki/Quaternary_glaciation "Quaternary glaciation") and on numerous previous occasions, the legacy of which can be seen in the extensive coverage of [drumlins](/wiki/Drumlin "Drumlin") in Counties Fermanagh, Armagh, Antrim and particularly Down. The centrepiece of Northern Ireland's geography is [Lough Neagh](/wiki/Lough_Neagh "Lough Neagh"), at 151 square miles (391 km2) the largest freshwater lake both on the island of Ireland and in the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"). A second extensive lake system is centred on Lower and Upper [Lough Erne](/wiki/Lough_Erne "Lough Erne") in Fermanagh. The largest island of Northern Ireland is [Rathlin](/wiki/Rathlin_Island "Rathlin Island"), off the north Antrim coast. [Strangford Lough](/wiki/Strangford_Lough "Strangford Lough") is the largest inlet in the British Isles, covering 150 km2 (58 sq mi).

There are substantial uplands in the [Sperrin Mountains](/wiki/Sperrin_Mountains "Sperrin Mountains") (an extension of the [Caledonian mountain belt](/wiki/Caledonian_orogeny "Caledonian orogeny")) with extensive gold deposits, the [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") [Mourne Mountains](/wiki/Mourne_Mountains "Mourne Mountains") and the [basalt](/wiki/Basalt "Basalt") [Antrim Plateau](/wiki/Antrim_Plateau "Antrim Plateau"), as well as smaller ranges in [South Armagh](/wiki/County_Armagh "County Armagh") and along the Fermanagh–Tyrone border. None of the hills are especially high, with [Slieve Donard](/wiki/Slieve_Donard "Slieve Donard") in the dramatic Mournes reaching 850 metres (2,789 ft), Northern Ireland's highest point. Belfast's most prominent peak is [Cavehill](/wiki/Cavehill "Cavehill"). The Lower and Upper [River Bann](/wiki/River_Bann "River Bann"), [River Foyle](/wiki/River_Foyle "River Foyle") and [River Blackwater](/wiki/River_Blackwater_(Northern_Ireland) "River Blackwater (Northern Ireland)") form extensive fertile lowlands, with excellent [arable land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land") also found in North and East Down, although much of the hill country is marginal and suitable largely for animal husbandry. The valley of the [River Lagan](/wiki/River_Lagan "River Lagan") is dominated by Belfast, whose metropolitan area includes over a third of the population of Northern Ireland, with heavy urbanisation and industrialisation along the Lagan Valley and both shores of [Belfast Lough](/wiki/Belfast_Lough "Belfast Lough").

### Climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Northern_Ireland_K%C3%B6ppen.png/250px-Northern_Ireland_K%C3%B6ppen.png)](/wiki/File:Northern_Ireland_K%C3%B6ppen.png)

[Köppen-Geiger climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") map of Northern Ireland

The vast majority of Northern Ireland has a [temperate maritime climate](/wiki/Maritime_climate "Maritime climate"), (*Cfb* in the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification")) rather wetter in the west than the east, although cloud cover is very common across the region. The weather is unpredictable at all times of the year, and although the seasons are distinct, they are considerably less pronounced than in interior Europe or the eastern seaboard of North America. Average daytime maximums in Belfast are 6.5 °C (43.7 °F) in January and 17.5 °C (63.5 °F) in July. The highest maximum temperature recorded was 31.4 °C (88.5 °F), registered in July 2021 at [Armagh Observatory](/wiki/Armagh_Observatory "Armagh Observatory")'s weather station.[[171]](#cite_note-176) The lowest minimum temperature recorded was −18.7 °C (−1.7 °F) at [Castlederg](/wiki/Castlederg "Castlederg"), [County Tyrone](/wiki/County_Tyrone "County Tyrone") on 23 December 2010.[[172]](#cite_note-177)

### Flora and fauna

Until the end of the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), the land was heavily forested. Native species include [deciduous](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") trees such as [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak"), [ash](/wiki/Fraxinus_excelsior "Fraxinus excelsior"), [hazel](/wiki/Corylus_avellana "Corylus avellana"), [birch](/wiki/Birch "Birch"), [alder](/wiki/Alnus_glutinosa "Alnus glutinosa"), [willow](/wiki/Willow "Willow"), [aspen](/wiki/Populus_tremula "Populus tremula"), [elm](/wiki/Wych_elm "Wych elm"), [rowan](/wiki/Sorbus_aucuparia "Sorbus aucuparia") and [hawthorn](/wiki/Crataegus_monogyna "Crataegus monogyna"), as well as [evergreen](/wiki/Evergreen "Evergreen") trees such [Scots pine](/wiki/Scots_pine "Scots pine"), [yew](/wiki/Taxus_baccata "Taxus baccata") and [holly](/wiki/Ilex_aquifolium "Ilex aquifolium").[[173]](#cite_note-178) Today, only 8% of Northern Ireland is woodland, and most of this is non-native [conifer](/wiki/Conifer "Conifer") [plantations](/wiki/Plantation "Plantation").[[174]](#cite_note-179)

As of the 21st century, Northern Ireland is the least forested part of the United Kingdom and Ireland, and one of the least forested countries in Europe.[[175]](#cite_note-180)

The only native reptile in Northern Ireland is the [viviparous lizard](/wiki/Viviparous_lizard "Viviparous lizard"), or common lizard, which is widely distributed, particularly in heaths, bogs and sand dunes. The [common frog](/wiki/Common_frog "Common frog") is a very widespread species. Some lakes support internationally important bird populations, [Lough Neagh](/wiki/Lough_Neagh "Lough Neagh") and [Lough Beg](/wiki/Lough_Beg "Lough Beg") hold up to 80,000 wintering waterfowl of some 20 species, including [ducks](/wiki/Duck "Duck"), [geese](/wiki/Geese "Geese"), [swans](/wiki/Swan "Swan") and [gulls](/wiki/Gull "Gull"). The [otter](/wiki/Eurasian_otter "Eurasian otter") is the fourth largest land mammal in Northern Ireland. It can be found along the river systems, although it is seldom seen and will avoid contact with humans.[[176]](#cite_note-Trust-181) 356 species of marine [algae](/wiki/Algae "Algae") have been recorded in the northeast of Ireland; 77 species are considered rare.[[177]](#cite_note-182)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Economy_of_Northern_Ireland "Economy of Northern Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Belfast_Titanic.jpg/250px-Belfast_Titanic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belfast_Titanic.jpg)

[Titanic Belfast](/wiki/Titanic_Belfast "Titanic Belfast"), in the [Titanic Quarter](/wiki/Titanic_Quarter "Titanic Quarter")

Northern Ireland traditionally had an industrial economy, most notably featuring shipbuilding, rope manufacture, and textiles. In 2019, 53% of GVA was generated by services, 22% by the public sector, 15% by production, 8% by construction and 2% by agriculture.[[178]](#cite_note-183)

In the coal industry, [John Kelly Limited](/wiki/John_Kelly_Limited "John Kelly Limited") was the largest coal firm in Northern Ireland, spanning 150 years.[[179]](#cite_note-184) It started in [Queen's Quay, Belfast](/wiki/Queen%27s_Quay,_Belfast "Queen's Quay, Belfast"), by [Samuel Kelly](/wiki/Samuel_Kelly_(coal_merchant) "Samuel Kelly (coal merchant)") as a coal commissions and grocer. After his death, son [John Kelly](/wiki/John_Kelly_(coal_merchant) "John Kelly (coal merchant)") took over and expanded the fleet of coasters. The business saw the most growth under John Kelly's son, [Sir Samuel Kelly](/wiki/Sir_Samuel_Kelly "Sir Samuel Kelly"), who incorporated the business as a [limited company](/wiki/Limited_company "Limited company") in 1911. John Kelly Limited expanded along the docks, likewise with their offices, expansion to Ballymena and Carrickfergus took place, and coal boats would travel to Larne Harbour. The company was renamed to Kelly's Fuels in the 1990s, and quickly became one of the biggest oil distributors in the Northern Ireland.[[180]](#cite_note-185)[[181]](#cite_note-186)

Belfast is the United Kingdom's second largest tech hub outside of London with more than 25% of their jobs being technology related. Many established multinational tech companies such as Fujitsu, SAP, IBM and Microsoft have a presence here. It is regarded an appealing place to live for tech professionals and has a low cost of living compared to other cities.[[182]](#cite_note-187)[[183]](#cite_note-188)

In 2019 Northern Ireland welcomed 5.3m visitors, who spent over £1billion. A total of 167 cruise ships docked at Northern Ireland ports in 2019.[[184]](#cite_note-189) Tourism in recent years has been a major growth area with key attractions including the Giants Causeway and the many castles in the region with the historic towns and cities of Belfast, Derry, Armagh and Enniskillen being popular with tourists. Entertainment venues include the SSE Arena, Waterfront Hall, the Grand Opera House and Custom House Square. Tourists use various means of transport around Northern Ireland such as vehicle hire, guided tours, taxi tours, electric bikes, electric cars and public transport.[[185]](#cite_note-190)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Harlandandwolffcranes.JPG/250px-Harlandandwolffcranes.JPG)](/wiki/File:Harlandandwolffcranes.JPG)

[Harland & Wolff](/wiki/Harland_%26_Wolff "Harland & Wolff") shipyard cranes named Samson and Goliath

Belfast currently has an 81-acre shipyard which was purposely developed to be able to take some of the world's largest vessels. It has the largest dry dock for ships in Europe measuring 556m x 93m and has 106m high cranes, it is ideally situated between the Atlantic Ocean and the North Sea.[[186]](#cite_note-191) The shipyard can build ships and complete maintenance contracts such as the contracts awarded by P&O and Cunard cruise ships in 2022.[[187]](#cite_note-192)

Northern Ireland feeds around 10 million people when their population is only 1.8 million.[[188]](#cite_note-193) The predominant activity on Northern Ireland farms in 2022 was cattle and sheep. 79 per cent of farms in Northern Ireland have some cattle, 38 per cent have some sheep. Over three-quarters of farms in Northern Ireland are very small, in 2022 there were 26,089 farms in Northern Ireland with approximately one million hectares of land farmed.[[189]](#cite_note-194) Northern Ireland is in a unique position where it can sell goods to the rest of the United Kingdom and the European Union tariff-free, free from customs declarations, rules of origin certificates and non-tariff barriers on the sale of goods to both regions.[[190]](#cite_note-195)[[191]](#cite_note-196)

Below is a comparison of the goods being sold and purchased between Northern Ireland and the United Kingdom, compared with the goods being exported and imported between Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland:

| --- | --- | --- |
| 2020 | £11.3 billion | £4.2 billion |
| 2021 | £12.8 billion | £5.2 billion |

### Infrastructure and transportation

Main article: [Transport in Ireland](/wiki/Transport_in_Ireland "Transport in Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Approaching_Seahill_station_-_geograph.org.uk_-_243422.jpg/250px-Approaching_Seahill_station_-_geograph.org.uk_-_243422.jpg)](/wiki/File:Approaching_Seahill_station_-_geograph.org.uk_-_243422.jpg)

An [NIR](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Railways "Northern Ireland Railways") [C3K](/wiki/NIR_Class_3000 "NIR Class 3000") [railcar](/wiki/Railcar "Railcar")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Entrance_to_George_Best_Belfast_City_Airport_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3145806.jpg/250px-Entrance_to_George_Best_Belfast_City_Airport_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3145806.jpg)](/wiki/File:Entrance_to_George_Best_Belfast_City_Airport_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3145806.jpg)

The terminal building of [Belfast City Airport](/wiki/Belfast_City_Airport "Belfast City Airport")

Northern Ireland has underdeveloped transport infrastructure, with most infrastructure concentrated around Greater Belfast, Greater Derry, and Craigavon. Northern Ireland is served by three airports—[Belfast International](/wiki/Belfast_International_Airport "Belfast International Airport") near [Antrim](/wiki/Antrim,_County_Antrim "Antrim, County Antrim"), [George Best Belfast City](/wiki/George_Best_Belfast_City_Airport "George Best Belfast City Airport") integrated into the railway network at [Sydenham](/wiki/Sydenham_railway_station,_Northern_Ireland "Sydenham railway station, Northern Ireland") in East Belfast, and [City of Derry](/wiki/City_of_Derry_Airport "City of Derry Airport") in County Londonderry. There are upgrade plans to transform the railway network in Northern Ireland including new lines from Derry to Portadown and Belfast to Newry, though it will take the best part of 25 years to deliver.[[193]](#cite_note-198) There are major seaports at [Larne](/wiki/Larne "Larne") and [Belfast](/wiki/Port_of_Belfast "Port of Belfast") which carry passengers and freight between Great Britain and Northern Ireland.

Passenger railways are operated by [NI Railways](/wiki/NI_Railways "NI Railways"). With [Iarnród Éireann](/wiki/Iarnr%C3%B3d_%C3%89ireann "Iarnród Éireann") (Irish Rail), [NI Railways](/wiki/NI_Railways "NI Railways") co-operates in providing the joint [Enterprise](/wiki/Enterprise_(train) "Enterprise (train)") service between [Dublin Connolly](/wiki/Dublin_Connolly "Dublin Connolly") and [Belfast Grand Central](/wiki/Belfast_Grand_Central_station "Belfast Grand Central station"). The whole of Ireland has a mainline railway network with a [gauge of 5 ft 3 in (1,600 mm)](/wiki/Track_gauge_in_Ireland "Track gauge in Ireland"), which is unique in Europe and has resulted in distinct rolling stock designs. The only preserved line of this gauge on the island is the [Downpatrick and County Down Railway](/wiki/Downpatrick_and_County_Down_Railway "Downpatrick and County Down Railway"), which operates heritage steam and diesel locomotives. Main railway lines linking to and from [Belfast Grand Central Station](/wiki/Belfast_Grand_Central_station "Belfast Grand Central station") and Lanyon Place railway station are:

* The [Derry Line](/wiki/Belfast%E2%80%93Derry_railway_line "Belfast–Derry railway line") and the Portrush Branch.
* The [Larne](/wiki/Larne_Town_railway_station "Larne Town railway station") Line
* The [Bangor](/wiki/Bangor_railway_station_(Northern_Ireland) "Bangor railway station (Northern Ireland)") Line
* The [Newry](/wiki/Newry_railway_station "Newry railway station")/[Portadown](/wiki/Portadown_railway_station "Portadown railway station") Line

The Derry line is the busiest single-track railway line in the United Kingdom, carrying 3 million passengers per annum, the Derry-Londonderry Line has also been described by Michael Palin as "one of the most beautiful rail journeys in the world".[[194]](#cite_note-199)

Main motorways are:

* [M1](/wiki/M1_motorway_(Northern_Ireland) "M1 motorway (Northern Ireland)") connecting Belfast to the south and west, ending in [Dungannon](/wiki/Dungannon "Dungannon")
* [M2](/wiki/M2_motorway_(Northern_Ireland) "M2 motorway (Northern Ireland)") connecting Belfast to the north. An unconnected section of the [M2](/wiki/M2_motorway_(Northern_Ireland) "M2 motorway (Northern Ireland)") also by-passes [Ballymena](/wiki/Ballymena "Ballymena")

Additional short motorway spurs include:

* [M12](/wiki/M12_motorway_(Northern_Ireland) "M12 motorway (Northern Ireland)") connecting the [M1](/wiki/M1_motorway_(Northern_Ireland) "M1 motorway (Northern Ireland)") to [Portadown](/wiki/Portadown "Portadown")
* [M22](/wiki/M22_motorway_(Northern_Ireland) "M22 motorway (Northern Ireland)") connecting the [M2](/wiki/M2_motorway_(Northern_Ireland) "M2 motorway (Northern Ireland)") to near [Randalstown](/wiki/Randalstown "Randalstown")
* [M3](/wiki/M3_motorway_(Northern_Ireland) "M3 motorway (Northern Ireland)") connecting the M1 (via the [A12](/wiki/Westlink_(road) "Westlink (road)")) and M2 in Belfast with the A2 dual carriageway to [Bangor](/wiki/Bangor,_County_Down "Bangor, County Down")
* [M5](/wiki/M5_motorway_(Northern_Ireland) "M5 motorway (Northern Ireland)") connecting Belfast to [Newtownabbey](/wiki/Newtownabbey "Newtownabbey")

The cross-border road connecting the ports of [Larne](/wiki/Larne "Larne") in Northern Ireland and [Rosslare Harbour](/wiki/Rosslare_Harbour "Rosslare Harbour") in the Republic of Ireland is being upgraded as part of an EU-funded scheme. [European route E01](/wiki/European_route_E01 "European route E01") runs from Larne through the island of Ireland, Spain, and Portugal to [Seville](/wiki/Seville "Seville").

## Demographics

Main articles: [Demographics of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Demographics_of_Northern_Ireland "Demographics of Northern Ireland") and [People of Northern Ireland](/wiki/People_of_Northern_Ireland "People of Northern Ireland")

### Counties

Main article: [Counties of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Counties_of_Northern_Ireland "Counties of Northern Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Northern_Ireland_-_Counties.png/250px-Northern_Ireland_-_Counties.png)](/wiki/File:Northern_Ireland_-_Counties.png)

The six counties of Northern Ireland

Northern Ireland consists of six historic [counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Ireland "Counties of Ireland"): [County Antrim](/wiki/County_Antrim "County Antrim"), [County Armagh](/wiki/County_Armagh "County Armagh"), [County Down](/wiki/County_Down "County Down"), [County Fermanagh](/wiki/County_Fermanagh "County Fermanagh"), [County Londonderry](/wiki/County_Londonderry "County Londonderry"),[[f]](#cite_note-200) and [County Tyrone](/wiki/County_Tyrone "County Tyrone").

These counties are no longer used for local government purposes; instead, there are eleven [districts of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Districts_of_Northern_Ireland "Districts of Northern Ireland") which have different geographical extents. These were created in 2015, replacing the twenty-six districts which previously existed.[[195]](#cite_note-201)

Although counties are no longer used for local governmental purposes, they remain a popular means of describing where places are. They are officially used while applying for an [Irish passport](/wiki/Irish_passport "Irish passport"), which requires one to state one's county of birth. The name of that county then appears in both Irish and English on the passport's information page, as opposed to the town or city of birth on the United Kingdom passport. The [Gaelic Athletic Association](/wiki/Gaelic_Athletic_Association "Gaelic Athletic Association") still uses the counties as its primary means of organisation and fields representative teams of each [GAA county](/wiki/GAA_county "GAA county"). The original system of [car registration numbers](/wiki/License_plates_of_Northern_Ireland "License plates of Northern Ireland") largely based on counties remains in use. In 2000, the [telephone numbering system](/wiki/Big_Number_Change#Northern_Ireland "Big Number Change") was restructured into an 8-digit scheme with (except for Belfast) the first digit approximately reflecting the county.

The county boundaries still appear on Ordnance Survey of Northern Ireland Maps and the Philip's Street Atlases, among others. With their decline in official use, there is often confusion surrounding towns and cities which lie near county boundaries, such as Belfast and [Lisburn](/wiki/Lisburn "Lisburn"), which are split between counties Down and Antrim (the majorities of both cities, however, are in Antrim).

In March 2018, *The Sunday Times* published its list of Best Places to Live in Britain, including the following places in Northern Ireland: [Ballyhackamore](/wiki/Ballyhackamore "Ballyhackamore") near Belfast (overall best for Northern Ireland); Holywood, County Down; Newcastle, County Down; Portrush, County Antrim; Strangford, County Down.[[196]](#cite_note-202)

### Population

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Northern_Ireland_%E2%80%93_religion_or_religion_brought_up_in_%28Christianity%3B_2011%29.png/250px-Northern_Ireland_%E2%80%93_religion_or_religion_brought_up_in_%28Christianity%3B_2011%29.png)](/wiki/File:Northern_Ireland_%E2%80%93_religion_or_religion_brought_up_in_(Christianity;_2011).png)

2011 census: differences in proportions of those who are, or were brought up, either Catholic or Protestant/Other Christians

The population of Northern Ireland has risen yearly since 1978. The population at the time of the 2021 census was 1.9 million, having grown 5% over the previous decade.[[199]](#cite_note-2021_census_main_points-206) The population in 2011 was 1.8 million, a rise of 7.5% over the previous decade.[[200]](#cite_note-Census_2011-207) The current population makes up 2.8% of the UK's population (67 million) and 27% of the island of Ireland's population (7.03 million). The population density is 135 inhabitants / km2.

Northern Ireland is much less ethnically diverse than the rest of the UK. As of the 2021 census, the population of Northern Ireland is almost entirely [white](/wiki/White_people "White people") (96.6%).[[201]](#cite_note-Ethnic_group_census_2022-208) 3.4% of the population (65,600 people) in 2025 were from a minority ethnic group compared to 18.3% in England and Wales and 12.9% in Scotland.[[202]](#cite_note-209) In 2021, 86.5% of the population were born in Northern Ireland, with 4.8% born in Great Britain, 2.1% born in the Republic of Ireland, and 6.5% born elsewhere (more than half of them in another European country).[[203]](#cite_note-Country_of_birth_census_2022-210) In 2021 the largest non-white ethnic groups were [black](/wiki/Black_people_in_Ireland "Black people in Ireland") (0.6%), [Indian](/wiki/Non-resident_Indian_and_person_of_Indian_origin "Non-resident Indian and person of Indian origin") (0.5%), and [Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") (0.5%).[[201]](#cite_note-Ethnic_group_census_2022-208) In 2011, 88.8% of the population were born in Northern Ireland, 4.5% in Great Britain, and 2.9% in the Republic of Ireland. 4.3% were born elsewhere; triple the amount there were in 2001.[[204]](#cite_note-211)

As of the 2021 Census 1,165,168 (61.2%) residents lived in an urban environment and 738,007 (38.8%) lived in a non-urban environment.[[205]](#cite_note-212)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Religion_in_Northern_Ireland "Religion in Northern Ireland")

At the 2021 census, 42.3% of the population identified as [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_Ireland "Roman Catholicism in Ireland"), 37.3% as Protestant/other Christian, 1.3% as other religions, while 17.4% identified with no religion or did not state one.[[215]](#cite_note-2021_religion-222) The biggest of the Protestant/other Christian denominations were the [Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Ireland "Presbyterian Church in Ireland") (16.6%), the [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") (11.5%) and the [Methodist Church](/wiki/Methodist_Church_in_Ireland "Methodist Church in Ireland") (2.3%).[[215]](#cite_note-2021_religion-222) At the [2011 census](/wiki/2011_United_Kingdom_census "2011 United Kingdom census"), 41.5% of the population identified as Protestant/other Christian, 41% as Roman Catholic, 0.8% as other religions, while 17% identified with no religion or did not state one.[[216]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-223) In terms of background (i.e. religion or religion brought up in), at the 2021 census 45.7% of the population came from a Catholic background, 43.5% from a Protestant background, 1.5% from other religious backgrounds, and 5.6% from non-religious backgrounds.[[215]](#cite_note-2021_religion-222) This was the first time since Northern Ireland's creation that there were more people from a Catholic background than Protestant.[[217]](#cite_note-first_catholic_majority-224) At the 2011 census, 48% came from a Protestant background, 45% from a Catholic background, 0.9% from other religious backgrounds, and 5.6% from non-religious backgrounds.[[216]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-223)

Several studies and surveys carried out between 1971 and 2006 have indicated that, in general, most Protestants in Northern Ireland see themselves primarily as British, whereas most Catholics see themselves primarily as Irish.[[220]](#cite_note-227)[[221]](#cite_note-228)[[222]](#cite_note-229)[[223]](#cite_note-230)[[224]](#cite_note-231)[[225]](#cite_note-232)[[226]](#cite_note-233)[[227]](#cite_note-234) This does not, however, account for the [complex identities within Northern Ireland](/wiki/People_of_Northern_Ireland "People of Northern Ireland"), given that many of the population regard themselves as "Ulster" or "Northern Irish", either as a primary or secondary identity.

A 2008 survey found that 57% of Protestants described themselves as British, while 32% identified as Northern Irish, 6% as Ulster, and 4% as Irish. Compared to a similar survey in 1998, this shows a fall in the percentage of Protestants identifying as British and Ulster and a rise in those identifying as Northern Irish. The 2008 survey found that 61% of Catholics described themselves as Irish, with 25% identifying as Northern Irish, 8% as British, and 1% as Ulster. These figures were largely unchanged from the 1998 results.[[228]](#cite_note-235)[[229]](#cite_note-236)

People born in Northern Ireland are, with some exceptions, deemed by UK law to be [citizens of the United Kingdom](/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law"). They are also, with similar exceptions, entitled to be [citizens of Ireland](/wiki/Irish_nationality_law "Irish nationality law"). This entitlement was reaffirmed in the 1998 [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement") between the British and Irish governments, which provides that:

> ...it is the birthright of all the people of Northern Ireland to identify themselves and be accepted as Irish or British, or both, as they may so choose, and accordingly [the two governments] confirm that their right to hold both British and Irish citizenship is accepted by both Governments and would not be affected by any future change in the status of Northern Ireland.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Predominant_passport_held_northern_ireland.png/250px-Predominant_passport_held_northern_ireland.png)](/wiki/File:Predominant_passport_held_northern_ireland.png)

2011: Map of most commonly held passport

As a result of the Agreement, the [Constitution of the Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ireland "Constitution of Ireland") was amended. The current wording provides that people born in Northern Ireland are entitled to be Irish citizens on the same basis as people from any other part of the island.[[230]](#cite_note-237)

Neither government, however, extends its citizenship to all persons born in Northern Ireland. Both governments exclude some people born in Northern Ireland, in particular persons born without one parent who is a British or Irish citizen. The Irish restriction was given effect by the [twenty-seventh amendment to the Irish Constitution](/wiki/Twenty-seventh_Amendment_of_the_Constitution_of_Ireland "Twenty-seventh Amendment of the Constitution of Ireland") in 2004. The position in UK nationality law is that most of those born in Northern Ireland are UK nationals, whether or not they so choose. Renunciation of British citizenship requires the payment of a fee, currently £372.[[231]](#cite_note-238)

In recent censuses, residents said they held the following passports:[[210]](#cite_note-2011_Census_data-217)[[232]](#cite_note-passport_held_2021-239)

| --- | --- | --- | --- |
