| United Kingdom | 59.1% | 52.6% |
| Ireland | 20.8% | 32.3% |
| European countries | 2.2% | 3.9% |
| Other countries in world | 1.1% | 1.6% |
| No passport | 18.9% | 15.9% |

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Languages_of_Northern_Ireland "Languages of Northern Ireland")

Irish is an official language of Northern Ireland as of 6 December 2022 when the Irish Language Act ([Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022](/wiki/Identity_and_Language_(Northern_Ireland)_Act_2022 "Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022")) became law. The Irish Language Act officially repealed legislation from 1737 that banned the use of Irish in courts.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) English is a *de facto* official language. English is spoken as a first language by 95.4% of the Northern Ireland population.[[233]](#cite_note-2022_Language-240)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/English_dialects_in_Ulster_contrast.png/250px-English_dialects_in_Ulster_contrast.png)](/wiki/File:English_dialects_in_Ulster_contrast.png)

Approximate boundaries of the current and historical English/[Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language") dialects in [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster"). South to north, the colour bands represent [Hiberno-English](/wiki/Hiberno-English "Hiberno-English"), South-[Ulster English](/wiki/Ulster_English "Ulster English"), Mid-Ulster English, and the three traditional [Ulster Scots](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_dialect "Ulster Scots dialect") areas. The Irish-speaking [Gaeltacht](/wiki/Gaeltacht "Gaeltacht") is not shown.

Under the [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/Belfast_Agreement "Belfast Agreement"), Irish and [Ulster Scots](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_dialect "Ulster Scots dialect") (an Ulster dialect of the [Scots language](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language"), sometimes known as *Ullans*), are recognised as "part of the cultural wealth of Northern Ireland".[[234]](#cite_note-241) The Irish Language Act of 2022 also legislated commissioners for both Irish and Ulster Scots.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

Two all-island bodies for the promotion of these were created under the Agreement: *[Foras na Gaeilge](/wiki/Foras_na_Gaeilge "Foras na Gaeilge")*, which promotes the Irish language, and the [Ulster Scots Agency](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_Agency "Ulster Scots Agency"), which promotes the Ulster-Scots dialect and culture. These operate separately under the aegis of [the North/South Language Body](/wiki/The_North/South_Language_Body "The North/South Language Body"), which reports to the [North/South Ministerial Council](/wiki/North/South_Ministerial_Council "North/South Ministerial Council").

The [UK Government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") in 2001 ratified the [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages"). Irish (in Northern Ireland) was specified under Part III of the Charter, with a range of specific undertakings about education, translation of statutes, interaction with public authorities, the use of placenames, media access, support for cultural activities, and other matters. A lower level of recognition was accorded to Ulster-Scots, under Part II of the Charter.[[235]](#cite_note-242)

#### English

Main article: [Ulster English](/wiki/Ulster_English "Ulster English")

According to the 2021 census, in 94.74% of households, all people aged 16 and above spoke English as their main language.[[236]](#cite_note-Census_2021_Phase_2_Bulletin-243) The dialect of English spoken in Northern Ireland shows influence from the lowland [Scots language](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language").[[237]](#cite_note-244) There are supposedly some minute differences in pronunciation between Protestants and Catholics, for instance; the name of the letter *h*, which Protestants tend to pronounce as "aitch", as in [British English](/wiki/British_English "British English"), and Catholics tend to pronounce as "haitch", as in [Hiberno-English](/wiki/Hiberno-English "Hiberno-English").[[238]](#cite_note-245) However, geography is a much more important determinant of dialect than religious background.

#### Irish

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Map_of_those_with_some_ability_in_Irish_in_the_2011_census_in_Northern_Ireland.png/250px-Map_of_those_with_some_ability_in_Irish_in_the_2011_census_in_Northern_Ireland.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_those_with_some_ability_in_Irish_in_the_2011_census_in_Northern_Ireland.png)

Percentage of people aged 3+ claiming to have some ability in Irish in the [2011 census](/wiki/2011_United_Kingdom_census "2011 United Kingdom census")

Main articles: [Irish language in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Irish_language_in_Northern_Ireland "Irish language in Northern Ireland") and [Ulster Irish](/wiki/Ulster_Irish "Ulster Irish")

The Irish language ([Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"): *an Ghaeilge*), or *Gaelic*, is the second most spoken language in Northern Ireland and is a native language of Ireland.[[239]](#cite_note-246) It was spoken predominantly throughout what is now Northern Ireland before the [Ulster Plantations](/wiki/Ulster_Plantations "Ulster Plantations") in the 17th century and most place names in Northern Ireland are [anglicised](/wiki/Anglicised "Anglicised") versions of a Gaelic name. Today, the language is often associated with Irish nationalism (and thus with Catholics). However, in the 19th century, the language was seen as a common heritage, with Ulster Protestants playing a leading role in the [Gaelic revival](/wiki/Gaelic_revival "Gaelic revival").[[240]](#cite_note-247)

In the 2021 census, 12.4% (compared with 10.7% in 2011) of the population of Northern Ireland claimed "some knowledge of Irish" and 3.9% (compared with 3.7% in 2011) reported being able to "speak, read, write and understand" Irish.[[200]](#cite_note-Census_2011-207)[[233]](#cite_note-2022_Language-240) In another survey, from 1999, 1% of respondents said they spoke it as their main language at home.[[241]](#cite_note-Survey-NorthernIreland-248)

The dialect spoken in Northern Ireland, Ulster Irish, has two main types, East Ulster Irish and Donegal Irish (or West Ulster Irish),[[242]](#cite_note-pipex1-249) is the one closest to [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic") (which developed into a separate language from Irish Gaelic in the 17th century). Some words and phrases are shared with Scots Gaelic, and the dialects of east Ulster – those of [Rathlin Island](/wiki/Rathlin_Island "Rathlin Island") and the [Glens of Antrim](/wiki/Glens_of_Antrim "Glens of Antrim") – were very similar to the dialect of [Argyll](/wiki/Argyll "Argyll"), the part of Scotland nearest to Ireland. The dialects of Armagh and Down were also very similar to the dialects of Galloway.

The use of the Irish language in Northern Ireland today is politically sensitive. The erection by some [district councils](/wiki/Local_government_in_Northern_Ireland "Local government in Northern Ireland") of bilingual street names in both English and Irish,[[243]](#cite_note-250) invariably in predominantly nationalist districts, is resisted by unionists who claim that it creates a "chill factor" and thus harms community relationships. Efforts by members of the [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") to legislate for some official uses of the language have failed to achieve the required cross-community support. In May 2022, the UK Government proposed a bill in the House of Lords to make Irish an official language (and support Ulster Scots) in Northern Ireland and to create an Irish Language Commissioner.[[244]](#cite_note-251)[[2]](#cite_note-Parliament3168-2) The bill has since been passed, and received royal assent in December 2022.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) There has recently been an increase in interest in the language among unionists in East Belfast.[[245]](#cite_note-252)

#### Ulster Scots

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Map_showing_the_percentage_of_people_aged_3%2B_claiming_to_have_some_ability_in_Ulster_Scots_in_the_2011_census.png/250px-Map_showing_the_percentage_of_people_aged_3%2B_claiming_to_have_some_ability_in_Ulster_Scots_in_the_2011_census.png)](/wiki/File:Map_showing_the_percentage_of_people_aged_3%2B_claiming_to_have_some_ability_in_Ulster_Scots_in_the_2011_census.png)

Percentage of people aged 3+ claiming to have some ability in Ulster Scots in the 2011 census

Main article: [Ulster Scots dialect](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_dialect "Ulster Scots dialect")

Ulster Scots comprises varieties of the [Scots language](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language") spoken in Northern Ireland. For a native English speaker, "[Ulster Scots] is comparatively accessible, and even at its most intense can be understood fairly easily with the help of a glossary."[[246]](#cite_note-Poilin-BBC-253)

Along with the Irish language, the Good Friday Agreement recognised the dialect as part of Northern Ireland's unique culture and the [St Andrews Agreement](/wiki/St_Andrews_Agreement "St Andrews Agreement") recognised the need to "enhance and develop the Ulster Scots language, heritage and culture".[[247]](#cite_note-254)

At the time of the 2021 census, approximately 1.1% (compared to 0.9% in 2011) of the population claimed to be able to speak, read, write and understand Ulster-Scots, while 10.4% (compared to 8.1% in 2011) professed to have "some ability".[[200]](#cite_note-Census_2011-207)[[233]](#cite_note-2022_Language-240)[[241]](#cite_note-Survey-NorthernIreland-248)

#### Sign languages

Main articles: [Northern Ireland Sign Language](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Sign_Language "Northern Ireland Sign Language"), [Irish Sign Language](/wiki/Irish_Sign_Language "Irish Sign Language"), and [British Sign Language](/wiki/British_Sign_Language "British Sign Language")

The most common [sign language](/wiki/Sign_language "Sign language") in Northern Ireland is [Northern Ireland Sign Language](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Sign_Language "Northern Ireland Sign Language") (NISL). However, because in the past Catholic families tended to send their deaf children to schools in Dublin[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] where [Irish Sign Language](/wiki/Irish_Sign_Language "Irish Sign Language") (ISL) is commonly used, ISL is still common among many older deaf people from Catholic families.

Irish Sign Language (ISL) has some influence from the French family of sign language, which includes [American Sign Language](/wiki/American_Sign_Language "American Sign Language") (ASL). NISL takes a large component from the British family of sign language (which also includes [Auslan](/wiki/Auslan "Auslan")) with many borrowings from ASL. It is described as being related to Irish Sign Language at the [syntactic](/wiki/Syntactic "Syntactic") level while much of the [lexicon](/wiki/Lexicon "Lexicon") is based on [British Sign Language](/wiki/British_Sign_Language "British Sign Language") (BSL).[[248]](#cite_note-Janzen-255)

As of March 2004[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Northern_Ireland&action=edit) the [UK Government](/wiki/British_Government "British Government") recognises only British Sign Language and Irish Sign Language as the official sign languages used in Northern Ireland.[[249]](#cite_note-dcal-256)[[250]](#cite_note-257)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Education_in_Northern_Ireland "Education in Northern Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/QueensBelfast.jpg/250px-QueensBelfast.jpg)](/wiki/File:QueensBelfast.jpg)

[Queen's University](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_Belfast "Queen's University Belfast"), [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast")

Unlike most areas of the United Kingdom, in the last year of primary school, many children sit entrance examinations for [grammar schools](/wiki/Grammar_schools_in_the_United_Kingdom "Grammar schools in the United Kingdom"). [Integrated schools](/wiki/Integrated_Education "Integrated Education"), which attempt to ensure a balance in enrolment between pupils of Protestant, Roman Catholic, and other faiths (or none), are becoming increasingly popular, although Northern Ireland still has a primarily *de facto* religiously segregated education system. In the primary school sector, 40 schools (8.9% of the total number) are integrated schools and 32 (7.2% of the total number) are [Gaelscoileanna](/wiki/Gaelscoileanna "Gaelscoileanna") (Irish language-medium schools).

As with the island of Ireland as a whole, Northern Ireland has one of the youngest populations in Europe and, among the four UK nations, it has the highest proportion of children aged under 16 years (21% in mid-2019).[[251]](#cite_note-258) Statistics on education in Northern Ireland are published by the [Department of Education](/wiki/Department_of_Education_(Northern_Ireland) "Department of Education (Northern Ireland)") and the [Department for the Economy](/wiki/Department_for_the_Economy "Department for the Economy").

The main universities in Northern Ireland are [Queen's University Belfast](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_Belfast "Queen's University Belfast") and [Ulster University](/wiki/Ulster_University "Ulster University"), and the distance learning [Open University](/wiki/Open_University "Open University") which has a regional office in Belfast.

### Health

See also: [Health and Social Care (Northern Ireland)](/wiki/Health_and_Social_Care_(Northern_Ireland) "Health and Social Care (Northern Ireland)")

Since 1948 Northern Ireland has a health care system similar to England, Scotland and Wales, though it provides not only health care, but also social care. Health care performance has been decreasing since the mid-2010s and reached crisis levels since 2022.[[252]](#cite_note-259)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Culture_of_Northern_Ireland "Culture of Northern Ireland")

Northern Ireland shares both the [culture of Ulster](/wiki/Culture_of_Ulster "Culture of Ulster") and the [culture of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Culture of the United Kingdom").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Belfast_waterfront.jpg/250px-Belfast_waterfront.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belfast_waterfront.jpg)

[SSE Arena](/wiki/Odyssey_Place "Odyssey Place") with 11,000 capacity on Belfast waterfront

Northern Ireland has witnessed rising numbers of tourists. Attractions include concert venues, cultural festivals, musical and artistic traditions, countryside and geographical sites of interest, public houses, welcoming hospitality, and sports (especially golf and fishing).[[253]](#cite_note-260) Since 1987 public houses have been allowed to open on Sundays, despite some opposition.

[Parades are a prominent feature](/wiki/Parades_in_Northern_Ireland "Parades in Northern Ireland") of Northern Ireland society,[[254]](#cite_note-261) more so than in the rest of Ireland or the United Kingdom. Most are held by Protestant fraternities such as the [Orange Order](/wiki/Orange_Order "Orange Order"), and Ulster loyalist marching bands. Each summer, during the "marching season", these groups have hundreds of parades, [deck streets with British flags](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_flags_issue "Northern Ireland flags issue"), bunting and specially-made arches, and light large towering bonfires in the ["Eleventh Night" celebrations](/wiki/Eleventh_Night "Eleventh Night").[[255]](#cite_note-bryan130-262) The biggest parades are held on 12 July ([The Twelfth](/wiki/The_Twelfth "The Twelfth")). There is often tension when these activities take place near Catholic neighbourhoods, which sometimes leads to violence.[[256]](#cite_note-263)

The [Ulster Cycle](/wiki/Ulster_Cycle "Ulster Cycle") is a large body of prose and verse centring on the traditional heroes of the [Ulaid](/wiki/Ulaid "Ulaid") in what is now eastern Ulster. This is one of the four major cycles of [Irish mythology](/wiki/Irish_mythology "Irish mythology"). The cycle centres on the reign of [Conchobar mac Nessa](/wiki/Conchobar_mac_Nessa "Conchobar mac Nessa"), who is said to have been the king of Ulster around the 1st century. He ruled from [Emain Macha](/wiki/Emain_Macha "Emain Macha") (now Navan Fort near Armagh), and had a fierce rivalry with queen [Medb](/wiki/Medb "Medb") and king Ailill of Connacht and their ally, [Fergus mac Róich](/wiki/Fergus_mac_R%C3%B3ich "Fergus mac Róich"), former king of Ulster. The foremost hero of the cycle is Conchobar's nephew [Cúchulainn](/wiki/C%C3%BAchulainn "Cúchulainn"), who features in the [epic prose/poem](/wiki/Epic_poetry "Epic poetry") *An [Táin Bó Cúailnge](/wiki/T%C3%A1in_B%C3%B3_C%C3%BAailnge "Táin Bó Cúailnge")* (The Cattle Raid of Cooley, a *[casus belli](/wiki/Casus_belli "Casus belli")* between Ulster and [Connaught](/wiki/Connaught "Connaught")).

### Symbols

See also: [Northern Ireland flags issue](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_flags_issue "Northern Ireland flags issue")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Flag_of_Northern_Ireland_%281924%E2%80%931953%29.svg/250px-Flag_of_Northern_Ireland_%281924%E2%80%931953%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Northern_Ireland_(1924%E2%80%931953).svg)

The [Ulster Banner](/wiki/Ulster_Banner "Ulster Banner") is used to represent Northern Ireland in [association football](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_national_football_team "Northern Ireland national football team") and at the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Logo_of_Northern_Ireland_Assembly.png/250px-Logo_of_Northern_Ireland_Assembly.png)](/wiki/File:Logo_of_Northern_Ireland_Assembly.png)

The logo for the [Northern Ireland assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") is based on the flower of the [flax](/wiki/Flax "Flax") plant.[[257]](#cite_note-NI_logo-264)

Northern Ireland comprises a patchwork of communities whose national loyalties are represented in some areas by flags flown from flagpoles or lamp posts. The [Union Jack](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack") and the former [Northern Ireland flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Northern_Ireland "Flag of Northern Ireland") are flown in many loyalist areas, and the Tricolour, adopted by republicans as the [flag of Ireland](/wiki/Flag_of_Ireland "Flag of Ireland") in 1916,[[258]](#cite_note-265) is flown in some republican areas. Even kerbstones in some areas are painted red-white-blue or green-white-orange, depending on whether local people express unionist/loyalist or nationalist/republican sympathies.[[259]](#cite_note-266)

The official flag is that of the state having sovereignty over the territory, i.e. the Union Flag.[[260]](#cite_note-267) The former Northern Ireland flag, also known as the "[Ulster Banner](/wiki/Ulster_Banner "Ulster Banner")" or "Red Hand Flag", is a banner derived from the coat of arms of the [Government of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Government_of_Northern_Ireland "Government of Northern Ireland") until 1972. Since 1972, it has had no official status. The Union Flag and the Ulster Banner are used exclusively by unionists. The UK flags policy states that in Northern Ireland, "The Ulster flag and the [Cross of St Patrick](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Saltire "Saint Patrick's Saltire") have no official status and, under the Flags Regulations, are not permitted to be flown from Government Buildings."[[261]](#cite_note-268)[[262]](#cite_note-269)

The [Irish Rugby Football Union](/wiki/Irish_Rugby_Football_Union "Irish Rugby Football Union") and the [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") have used the [Saint Patrick's Saltire](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Saltire "Saint Patrick's Saltire") or "Cross of St Patrick". This red [saltire](/wiki/Saltire "Saltire") on a white field was used to represent Ireland in the [flag of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom "Flag of the United Kingdom"). It is still used by some [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") regiments. Foreign flags are also found, such as the [Palestinian](/wiki/Palestinian_territories "Palestinian territories") flags in some nationalist areas and [Israeli](/wiki/Israel "Israel") flags in some unionist areas.[[263]](#cite_note-270)

The United Kingdom national anthem of "[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")" is often played at state events in Northern Ireland. At the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games") and some other sporting events, the Northern Ireland team uses the Ulster Banner as its flag—notwithstanding its lack of official status—and the "[Londonderry Air](/wiki/Londonderry_Air "Londonderry Air")" (usually set to [lyrics](/wiki/Lyrics "Lyrics") as "[Danny Boy](/wiki/Danny_Boy "Danny Boy")"), which also has no official status, as its [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem").[[264]](#cite_note-271)[[265]](#cite_note-272) The [Northern Ireland national football team](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_national_football_team "Northern Ireland national football team") also uses the Ulster Banner as its flag but uses "God Save The King" as its anthem.[[266]](#cite_note-273) Major [Gaelic Athletic Association](/wiki/Gaelic_Athletic_Association "Gaelic Athletic Association") matches are opened by the national anthem of the Republic of Ireland, "[Amhrán na bhFiann](/wiki/Amhr%C3%A1n_na_bhFiann "Amhrán na bhFiann") (The Soldier's Song)", which is also used by most other all-Ireland sporting organisations.[[267]](#cite_note-274) Since 1995, the [Ireland rugby union team](/wiki/Ireland_rugby_union_team "Ireland rugby union team") has used a specially commissioned song, "[Ireland's Call](/wiki/Ireland%27s_Call "Ireland's Call")" as the team's anthem. The Irish national anthem is also played at Dublin home matches, being the anthem of the host country.[[268]](#cite_note-275)

[Northern Irish murals](/wiki/Northern_Irish_murals "Northern Irish murals") have become well-known features of Northern Ireland, depicting past and present events and documenting peace and cultural diversity. Almost 2,000 murals have been documented in Northern Ireland since the 1970s.

### Media and communications

Main articles: [Media of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Media_of_Northern_Ireland "Media of Northern Ireland") and [Mass media in the Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Mass_media_in_the_Republic_of_Ireland "Mass media in the Republic of Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/BBC_Broadcasting_House%2C_Belfast%2C_October_2010_%2801%29.JPG/250px-BBC_Broadcasting_House%2C_Belfast%2C_October_2010_%2801%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:BBC_Broadcasting_House,_Belfast,_October_2010_(01).JPG)

[Broadcasting House, Belfast](/wiki/Broadcasting_House,_Belfast "Broadcasting House, Belfast"), home of [BBC Northern Ireland](/wiki/BBC_Northern_Ireland "BBC Northern Ireland")

The [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") has a division called [BBC Northern Ireland](/wiki/BBC_Northern_Ireland "BBC Northern Ireland") with headquarters in Belfast and operates [BBC One Northern Ireland](/wiki/BBC_One_Northern_Ireland "BBC One Northern Ireland") and [BBC Two Northern Ireland](/wiki/BBC_Two_Northern_Ireland "BBC Two Northern Ireland"). As well as broadcasting standard UK-wide programmes, BBC NI produces local content, including a news break-out called [BBC Newsline](/wiki/BBC_Newsline "BBC Newsline"). The [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)") franchise in Northern Ireland is [UTV](/wiki/UTV_(TV_channel) "UTV (TV channel)"). The state-owned [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4") and the privately owned [Channel 5](/wiki/Channel_5_(British_TV_channel) "Channel 5 (British TV channel)") also broadcast in Northern Ireland. Access is also available to satellite and cable services.[[269]](#cite_note-276) All Northern Ireland viewers must obtain a UK [TV licence](/wiki/Television_licensing_in_the_United_Kingdom "Television licensing in the United Kingdom") to watch live television transmissions or use [BBC iPlayer](/wiki/BBC_iPlayer "BBC iPlayer").

[RTÉ](/wiki/RT%C3%89 "RTÉ"), the national broadcaster of the Republic of Ireland, is available over the air to most parts of Northern Ireland via reception overspill of the Republic's [Saorview](/wiki/Saorview "Saorview") service,[[270]](#cite_note-277) or via satellite and cable. Since the digital TV switchover, [RTÉ One](/wiki/RT%C3%89_One "RTÉ One"), [RTÉ2](/wiki/RT%C3%892 "RTÉ2") and the Irish-language channel [TG4](/wiki/TG4 "TG4"), are now available over the air on the UK's [Freeview](/wiki/Freeview_(UK) "Freeview (UK)") system from transmitters within Northern Ireland.[[271]](#cite_note-278) Although they are transmitted in standard definition, a Freeview HD box or television is required for reception.

As well as the standard UK-wide radio stations from the BBC, Northern Ireland is home to many local radio stations, such as [Cool FM](/wiki/Cool_FM "Cool FM"), [Q Radio](/wiki/Q_Radio_Network "Q Radio Network"), [Downtown Radio](/wiki/Downtown_Radio "Downtown Radio") and [U105](/wiki/U105 "U105"). The BBC has two regional radio stations which broadcast in Northern Ireland, [BBC Radio Ulster](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Ulster "BBC Radio Ulster") and [BBC Radio Foyle](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Foyle "BBC Radio Foyle").

Besides the UK and Irish national newspapers, there are three main regional newspapers published in Northern Ireland. These are the *[Belfast Telegraph](/wiki/Belfast_Telegraph "Belfast Telegraph")*, *[The Irish News](/wiki/The_Irish_News "The Irish News")* and *[The News Letter](/wiki/The_News_Letter "The News Letter")*.[[272]](#cite_note-279) According to the [Audit Bureau of Circulations (UK)](/wiki/Audit_Bureau_of_Circulations_(UK) "Audit Bureau of Circulations (UK)") the average daily circulation for these three titles in 2018 was:

:   | Title | Market type | Print time | Political alignment | Format | Circulation Jan–Jun 2018[[273]](#cite_note-ABC_Northern_Ireland-280) | Circulation Jul–Dec 2018[[273]](#cite_note-ABC_Northern_Ireland-280) |
    | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
    | *[Belfast Telegraph](/wiki/Belfast_Telegraph "Belfast Telegraph")* | Regional | Morning | Non-Sectarianism/British Unionism | Compact | 35,931 | 33,951 |
    | *[The Irish News](/wiki/The_Irish_News "The Irish News")* | Regional | Morning | Irish nationalism | Compact | 33,647 | 32,315 |
    | *[News Letter](/wiki/News_Letter "News Letter")* | Regional | Morning | British unionism | Tabloid | 13,374 | 12,499 |

Northern Ireland uses the same telecommunications and postal services as the rest of the United Kingdom at standard domestic rates and there are no mobile roaming charges between Great Britain and Northern Ireland.[[274]](#cite_note-281)[[275]](#cite_note-282) People in Northern Ireland who live close to the border with the Republic of Ireland may inadvertently switch over to the Irish mobile networks, causing international roaming fees to be applied.[[276]](#cite_note-283) Calls from landlines in Northern Ireland to numbers in the Republic of Ireland are charged at the same rate as those to numbers in Great Britain, while landline numbers in Northern Ireland can similarly be called from the Republic of Ireland at domestic rates, using the [048](/wiki/Telephone_numbers_in_the_Republic_of_Ireland#Calls_to_Northern_Ireland "Telephone numbers in the Republic of Ireland") prefix.[[277]](#cite_note-284)

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Sport_in_Northern_Ireland "Sport in Northern Ireland")

#### Organisation of sports

Many sports are organised on an [all-Ireland](/wiki/All-Ireland "All-Ireland") basis, with a single governing body or team for the whole island.[[278]](#cite_note-theherald1-285) The most notable exception is association football (soccer), which has a separate governing body, league and national team for Northern Ireland.[[278]](#cite_note-theherald1-285)[[279]](#cite_note-286)

#### Football

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Scotland_v_n_ireland.jpg/250px-Scotland_v_n_ireland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scotland_v_n_ireland.jpg)

The [Northern Ireland football team](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_national_football_team "Northern Ireland national football team") line up with the [Scotland national team](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") in football

The [Irish Football Association](/wiki/Irish_Football_Association "Irish Football Association") (IFA) serves as the organising body for men's domestic and national association football in Northern Ireland, it is a member of the [International Football Association Board](/wiki/International_Football_Association_Board "International Football Association Board") which sets the rules for association football.[[280]](#cite_note-287) The [NIWFA](/wiki/NIWFA "NIWFA") are responsible for women's domestic and national association football in Northern Ireland.

The [NIFL Premiership](/wiki/NIFL_Premiership "NIFL Premiership") is a professional men's football league which operates at the highest division of the [Northern Ireland Football League](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Football_League "Northern Ireland Football League"), the current format has been organised with 12 clubs. The winners will enter the first qualifying round of the [Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"), if they do not progress they will enter the [Europa League](/wiki/Europa_League "Europa League") or [Europa Conference League](/wiki/Europa_Conference_League "Europa Conference League") depending on performance. The two runners-up progress to the Europa Conference League with play-offs for another Europa Conference League position.[[281]](#cite_note-288) The [NLFL Women's Premiership](/wiki/Women%27s_Premiership_(Northern_Ireland) "Women's Premiership (Northern Ireland)") is a professional women's football league which operates at the highest division in Northern Ireland with 10 clubs. The winner qualifies for a spot in the [UEFA Women's Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Women%27s_Champions_League "UEFA Women's Champions League"). The men's [Northern Ireland national football team](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_national_football_team "Northern Ireland national football team") qualified for the [1958 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1958_FIFA_World_Cup "1958 FIFA World Cup"), [1982 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1982_FIFA_World_Cup "1982 FIFA World Cup") and [1986 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup "1986 FIFA World Cup"), making it to the quarter-finals in 1958 and 1982 and made it the first knockout round in the [European Championships](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship") in 2016.

#### Rugby

The [IRFU](/wiki/Irish_Rugby_Football_Union "Irish Rugby Football Union") is the governing body for the sport of Rugby Union on the island of Ireland (Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland).[[282]](#cite_note-289) Rugby in Northern Ireland is run within the historic province of [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster") which includes Northern Ireland plus 3 counties from the Republic of Ireland - Donegal, Cavan and Monaghan.

The [Ireland national rugby league team](/wiki/Ireland_national_rugby_league_team "Ireland national rugby league team") has participated in the [Emerging Nations Tournament](/wiki/Rugby_League_Emerging_Nations_Tournament "Rugby League Emerging Nations Tournament") (1995), the Super League World Nines (1996), the World Cup (2000, 2008, 2013, 2017, 2021), European Nations Cup (since 2003) and Victory Cup (2004). The Ireland A rugby league team competes annually in the Amateur Four Nations competition (since 2002) and the St Patrick's Day Challenge (since 1995).

#### Cricket and golf

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Rory_McIlroy_watches_drive_flight_%28crowd%2C_landscape_orientation%29.jpg/250px-Rory_McIlroy_watches_drive_flight_%28crowd%2C_landscape_orientation%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rory_McIlroy_watches_drive_flight_(crowd,_landscape_orientation).jpg)

Prominent Northern Irish golfer [Rory McIlroy](/wiki/Rory_McIlroy "Rory McIlroy")

The [Ireland cricket team](/wiki/Ireland_cricket_team "Ireland cricket team") represents both Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland. It is a full member of the [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council"), having been granted [Test status](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") and full membership by the ICC in June 2017. The side competes in Test cricket, the highest level of competitive cricket in the international arena, and is one of the 12 full-member countries of the ICC. Ireland men's side has played in the [Cricket World Cup](/wiki/Ireland_at_the_Cricket_World_Cup "Ireland at the Cricket World Cup") and [T20 World Cup](/wiki/ICC_Men%27s_T20_World_Cup "ICC Men's T20 World Cup") and has won the [ICC Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "ICC Intercontinental Cup") four times. The [women's side](/wiki/Ireland_women%27s_cricket_team "Ireland women's cricket team") has played in the [Women's World Cup](/wiki/Women%27s_Cricket_World_Cup "Women's Cricket World Cup"). One of the men's side's regular international venues is [Stormont](/wiki/Stormont_(cricket_ground) "Stormont (cricket ground)") in Belfast.

The governing body for golf on the island of Ireland is [Golf Ireland](/wiki/Golf_Ireland "Golf Ireland"), it is the successor to the Golfing Union of Ireland, governing body for men's and boy's amateur golf, and the oldest golfing union in the world, which was founded in Belfast in 1891, and the Irish Ladies Golf Union. Northern Ireland's golf courses include the [Royal Belfast Golf Club](/wiki/Royal_Belfast_Golf_Club "Royal Belfast Golf Club") (the earliest, formed in 1881), [Royal Portrush Golf Club](/wiki/Royal_Portrush_Golf_Club "Royal Portrush Golf Club"), which is the only course outside Great Britain to have hosted [The Open Championship](/wiki/The_Open_Championship "The Open Championship"), and [Royal County Down Golf Club](/wiki/Royal_County_Down_Golf_Club "Royal County Down Golf Club") (*[Golf Digest](/wiki/Golf_Digest "Golf Digest")* magazine's top-rated course outside the United States).[[283]](#cite_note-290)[[284]](#cite_note-291) Northern Ireland had three major champions in the space of just 14 months from the [U.S. Open](/wiki/U.S._Open_(golf) "U.S. Open (golf)") in [2010](/wiki/2010_U.S._Open_(golf) "2010 U.S. Open (golf)") to [The Open Championship](/wiki/The_Open_Championship "The Open Championship") in [2011](/wiki/2011_Open_Championship "2011 Open Championship"). Notable golfers include [Fred Daly](/wiki/Fred_Daly_(golfer) "Fred Daly (golfer)") (winner of The Open in [1947](/wiki/1947_Open_Championship "1947 Open Championship")), [Ryder Cup](/wiki/Ryder_Cup "Ryder Cup") players [Ronan Rafferty](/wiki/Ronan_Rafferty "Ronan Rafferty") and [David Feherty](/wiki/David_Feherty "David Feherty"), leading [European Tour](/wiki/European_Tour "European Tour") professionals [David Jones](/wiki/David_Jones_(golfer) "David Jones (golfer)"), [Michael Hoey](/wiki/Michael_Hoey_(golfer) "Michael Hoey (golfer)") (a five-time winner on the tour) and [Gareth Maybin](/wiki/Gareth_Maybin "Gareth Maybin"), as well as three recent major winners [Graeme McDowell](/wiki/Graeme_McDowell "Graeme McDowell") (winner of the U.S. Open in 2010, the first European to do so since 1970), [Rory McIlroy](/wiki/Rory_McIlroy "Rory McIlroy") (winner of five [majors](/wiki/Men%27s_major_golf_championships "Men's major golf championships")) and [Darren Clarke](/wiki/Darren_Clarke "Darren Clarke") (winner of The Open in 2011).[[285]](#cite_note-292)[[286]](#cite_note-293) Northern Ireland has also contributed several players to the Great Britain and Ireland [Walker Cup](/wiki/Walker_Cup "Walker Cup") team, including [Alan Dunbar](/wiki/Alan_Dunbar "Alan Dunbar") and Paul Cutler who played on the victorious 2011 team in Scotland. Dunbar also won [The Amateur Championship](/wiki/The_Amateur_Championship "The Amateur Championship") in 2012, at Royal Troon.

14. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-alphabeticalNI_18-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-alphabeticalNI_18-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-alphabeticalNI_18-2) Dunn, S.; H. Dawson (2000), *An Alphabetical Listing of Word, Name and Place in Northern Ireland and the Living Language of Conflict*, [Lewiston, New York](/wiki/Lewiston,_New_York "Lewiston, New York"): [Edwin Mellen Press](/wiki/Edwin_Mellen_Press "Edwin Mellen Press"), "One specific problem – in both general and particular senses – is to know what to call Northern Ireland itself: in the general sense, it is not a country, or a province, or a state – although some refer to it contemptuously as a statelet: the least controversial word appears to be jurisdiction, but this might change."
15. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-interpretingNI_19-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-interpretingNI_19-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-interpretingNI_19-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-interpretingNI_19-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-interpretingNI_19-4) Whyte, J.; G. FitzGerald (1991), *Interpreting Northern Ireland*, Oxford: Oxford University Press, "One problem must be adverted to in writing about Northern Ireland. This is the question of what name to give to the various geographical entities. These names can be controversial, with the choice often revealing one's political preferences. ... some refer to Northern Ireland as a 'province'. That usage can arouse irritation particularly among nationalists, who claim the title 'province' should be properly reserved to the four historic provinces of Ireland-Ulster, Leinster, Munster, and Connacht. If I want to a label to apply to Northern Ireland I shall call it a 'region'. Unionists should find that title as acceptable as 'province': Northern Ireland appears as a region in the regional statistics of the United Kingdom published by the British government."
16. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-placeApart_20-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-placeApart_20-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-placeApart_20-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-placeApart_20-3) Murphy, D. (1979), *[A Place Apart](/wiki/A_Place_Apart_(Murphy_book) "A Place Apart (Murphy book)")*, London: Penguin Books, "Next – what noun is appropriate to Northern Ireland? 'Province' won't do since one-third of the province is on the wrong side of the border. 'State' implies more self-determination than Northern Ireland has ever had and 'country' or 'nation' are blatantly absurd. 'Colony' has overtones that would be resented by both communities and 'statelet' sounds too patronizing, though outsiders might consider it more precise than anything else; so one is left with the unsatisfactory word 'region'."

258. **[^](#cite_ref-265)** Alan O'Day, ed. (1987). *Reactions To Irish Nationalism, 1865–1914*. London: Hambledon Press. "With the Rising of 1916 a break took place in national symbolism which was most visibly manifested in the national flag and the anthem which the young Irish nation accepted. The demise of the Parliamentary Party stands in direct parallel to the just as rapidly diminishing power of its symbols. The green flag and 'God save Ireland' began to be discredited as symbols of constitutional nationalism and, instead, the symbols of revolutionary nationalism gained popularity as the majority of the Irish people identified themselves with the political aims of the Easter revolutionaries. The use of symbols made apparent that the occurrences of 1916 initiated a new epoch in Irish history much in the same as the Union of 1801 and the Famine of 1845–8 did. Both the national flag and the national anthem of present-day Ireland drive origins directly from the Rising. At first, it still appeared as if the revolutionaries would take over the old symbols because on the roof of their headquarters, the Dublin General Post Office, a green flag with the harp was hoisted next to the republican tricolour although with the inscription 'Irish Republic'. Even 'Got save Ireland' was sung by the revolutionaries during Easter week. But after the failure of the Rising and the subsequent executions of the leading revolutionaries the tricolour and 'The Soldier's Song' became more and more popular as symbols of the rebellion."
